As I read the code a bit more, I see that the bot is programmed to repeatedly reconnect, following the `recon` strategy. If the exception were allowed to pass, it would crash the server rather than follow the reconnection strategy. Perhaps the exception should be trapped in jump_server or in another part of the logic.

There may be a better way to handle this intention, but I'm fairly certain removing that exception trap will cause issues.

If I create a clean virtual environment on my computer and run `pip install gdbgui --upgrade` it installs with no problem. 

This seems to be some kind of environment issue, not something specific to gdbgui. Could you provide instructions for me to recreate the error? Did you use the `--upgrade` flag when using `pip`?

Hi thanks for reporting this.

gdbgui sends commands "behind the scenes" to try to make the experience as good as possible. Sometimes they need some fine tuning. This might be the case here since it looks like you're using nim, which I did not know could be used with gdb (or even existed 😄 ).

Regarding `set breakpoint pending on` that command is sent by gdbgui automatically. There is no option to send/not send it though. I should probably add one to turn it on/off.

I suggest going to settings (gear icon in top right) and unchecking `Add breakpoint to main after loading executable`.

I suspect this breakpoint is added to the address of the main function in a way that gdb could not determine the associated source file, so it puts you in some weird low-level stack at that address and maybe is the source of the "unable to read memory" errors.

See if that helps. If not, go back to settings and check `Print all sent commands in console, including those sent automatically by gdbgui`. Then reproduce all of the spam you get in the console. That will let us see what commands gdbgui is sending and hopefully determine the root cause of the messages.

I'm tinkering with the notion of expanding the dbus native 'signal' idea in an expanded pydbus implementation, which would enable the ideas above.  See the fork hcoin/pydbus The working notion is:  if any property or method call appends an  '_async' then it completes immediately returning ever only 'none', with the timeout or completion or error noted in the arguments to function passed at bus.get time.

That, and named arguments. And defaults.  And interpretation of bits in integers as tuples of string flags, and, bug fixes that allow pydbus to work on all the major distros, including publishing on glib versions prior to 2.46.  All demonstrated with travis CI examples.

And... the novel notion that a dbus services can often be thought of as it's own ecosystem, one that uses argument names consistently within the namespace of a service.  So... there's an option that supplies argument defaults from the last known value of that argument name in any use within that space.

That, and:   result=dbuservice.method(<whatnot>)  -->  say result position1 has name 'one'

result[1]==result.one==result['one']==result._arg_one == dbuservice._state.one

See much of it at https://github.com/hcoin/pydbus .  I've got a big commit coming that implements the service-global argument-name-as-attribute space.

Pydbus is a *great* idea from Linus.   My work is to make it possible for broad acceptance by doing much of the distro specific legwork and adding a few ideas.

By default augmenters resize images after augmentation so that the size stays the same with respect to the original input size. If you don't want this, use e.g. `CropAndPad(..., keep_size=False)`. Then images should change in size.

You can save images using e.g. `scipy.misc.imsave()`.
```python
from scipy import misc
filepath = "/tmp/images/%d.jpg"
images_aug = seq.augment_images(images)
for i, image_aug in enumerate(images_aug):
    misc.imsave(filepath % (i,), image_aug)
```

Do we really want `.pew`?

I mean: hidden files/directories are especially useful when you want to have something out of sight. But if an user is manually and explictily looking inside the `$WORKON_HOME/$VIRTUAL_ENV` that configuration is probably what he's looking for.

Also: compare with other things inside `.config` and `.local/share`: the subfolders aren't hidden: to get there in the first place the user had to get into an hidden folder (and by default, pew is using an hidden folder as well, that same `.local/share`). Contrast with `.project`, that is an automatically created file that users probably never have to touch themselves.

This leave the actual name of the folder to pick. Since the virtualenv is replicating the filesystem hierachy structure, I'd store things in `etc/`, but this cannot apply for the global hooks/env variables... a generic non-hidden `pew` might cut it?

Thank you again!

On Mon, May 4, 2015 at 11:22 AM, Steve Ivy <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> From what I've seen, upon launch Will starts 3 new processes - one each for the scheduler, XMPP, and web server (bottle). Normally on `KeyboardInterupt` or `SystemExit`, Will stops each of these threads cleanly.
> I've been experimenting with running Will under [envconsul](https://github.com/hashicorp/envconsul), which watches and loads configs from the Consul KV store and inserts them into the ENV before starting Will. The trouble seems to be that when changes occur to the Consul KV data, envconsul sends a `SIGTERM` to Will, which ends up killing the original process, but not the three spawned processes. envconsul then restarts Will, which spawns 3 new processes, leaving the original three running.
> I did take a hack at a patch but I'm crap at threading code, and have not yet made it work. Expect it will have to do with registering a `SIGTERM` handler (correctly) then shutting things down when it's fired.

Interesting question. Presently, I have not covered this circumstance: FoBiS.py recognizes static or shared external library, but not precompiled mod files. Indeed, precompiled mod are not well portable: each compiler vendor has its own mod format, the mod being not standard. Consequently, if you try use a mod compiled with intel by the gnu compiler (for example) this should not work. Anyhow, within the same compiler (maybe it is necessary also the same architecture) mod files are portable and FoBiS.py should be able to use them.

To try to implement this feature it would be useful to have "prototype scenario". Let us assume thatproject tree is the following:

```bash
src => foo.f90
mod => bar.mod
obj => bar.o
```
 
To build `foo.f90` the precompiled module `bar` is necessary. Does this scenary fit your request?

@inytar 
I read from official doc [18.5.1](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-eventloop.html), the AbstractEventLoop is not thread safe. But `asyncio.get_event_loop` will use `BaseDefaultEventLoopPolicy` as default.
```
class BaseDefaultEventLoopPolicy(AbstractEventLoopPolicy):
    """Default policy implementation for accessing the event loop.

    In this policy, each thread has its own event loop.  However, we
    only automatically create an event loop by default for the main
    thread; other threads by default have no event loop.

    Other policies may have different rules (e.g. a single global
    event loop, or automatically creating an event loop per thread, or
    using some other notion of context to which an event loop is
    associated).
    """

    _loop_factory = None
```
According to annotation in `BaseDefaultEventLoopPolicy`, it will create a new event loop when called in different thread.
`asyncio.get_event_loop` will also help if user customize different event loop for them self.
So i think the best way is to use somthing like `loop = None or asyncio.get_event_loop()` to pass loop to connection pool

Note that `./app.py help import xml` also works but `./app.py import help xml` doesn't.

Current implementation: actually there's no command `help`, it's always just a very simple top-level alias to `./app.py import xml --help`. See https://github.com/neithere/argh/blob/master/argh/dispatching.py#L138

It works but it's not extensible. Fiddling with HelpFormatter is not a good idea because only its name is considered public API (see the argparse code).

We *may* be able to do it by adding a _HelpAction or a similar object to the parser. However, I'm not sure how exactly it should be done.

pdfrw wasn't really designed for text extraction or manipulation.  If that's what you want, you might try pdfminer.

But...

If you really don't care whether a determined foe can reach into the PDF and figure out what the word is that you've redacted, and you know the coordinates and size of the word (or, to be more specific, you want to do this repeatedly at the same coordinates, so that it's worthwhile to figure those out), then pdfrw can certainly allow you to place a rectangle anywhere you want on a page.

If you look at the fancy_watermark examples, you will see how you can combine whole pages like this.  The pagemerge module that the example uses allows you to place one page (or a rectangle from a page) anywhere you want on top of another page.

If I were doing this, I would simply start by using something like inkscape to make a page (that could be any size, really) all one color.  But if you need white, make sure it's not transparent.   Then "watermark" your other page with that page, and play with the x/y coordinates of the watermark.

```python
import numpy as np
from mlxtend.classifier import StackingClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

# Generate some data
X, y = make_blobs(random_state=2)
# Build a StackingClassifier
classifiers=[RandomForestClassifier(random_state=2),
             SGDClassifier(random_state=2)]
meta_classifier = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=2)
sclf = StackingClassifier(classifiers=classifiers, meta_classifier=meta_classifier)
# Define some fit_params

This one took a while to track down.

It all comes down to the fact that modern versions of pip no longer support Python 3.2.  As in, pip doesn't actually function correctly on Python 3.2 any more.

That particular test installs the latest virtualenv and then runs the test inside the virtualenv.  The virtualenv installs and uses the latest version of pip.  pypy3 is stuck emulating Python 3.2.5 (I'm honestly bewildered that they haven't bumped up emulation to a later version by now, they've been on that version for many years).

So, pypy3 is too old to support.  I never supported Python 3.2 anyway.  pypy3 just happened to work well, so I supported it.  Until now.

Time to drop pypy3 support until they can target 3.4+.

My advice to you would be, add a commit that removes all pypy3 stuff.

Here's an idea to get package managers like `pip` to work behind a PAC network.

Find your PAC file, and open it to find out what the actual proxy server is. Then define the `HTTP_PROXY` and `HTTPS_PROXY` environment variables, giving them a value following the pattern `http://user:password@proxy-host:port`. `pip` should honour this environment variable. This way, by specifying an actual proxy, you avoid the problem of trying to get `pip` to recognize PACs.

The username and password in that environment variable will only work with proxies that require Basic authentication.

It's been a long time, but I dug up this test case that I believe was triggering the problem. It's a little arcane (and may no longer work), but the gist is you run a bunch of workers which all read some random bytes from yas3fs, then, while they're running, you reset the caches.  Before this patch, they were locking up every time.

```
#!/bin/bash

trap "trap - SIGTERM && kill -- -$$" SIGINT SIGTERM EXIT

set -e

if [ "$#" -ne 5 ]; then
    echo "readrandom topicqueuename bucket filename chunksize workers"
    echo
    exit 1
fi

if ! hash aws; then
    sudo pip install awscli --ignore-installed six
fi

if hash sha1sum 2>/dev/null; then
    checksum=sha1sum
else
    checksum=shasum
fi

topicqueue=$1
bucket=$2
input=$3
size=$4
workers=$5

if pgrep -f yas3fs; then
    pgrep -f yas3fs | xargs sudo kill -9
    sudo fusermount -uz /myapp/data
fi
sudo rm -f /var/log/yas3fs.log;

sudo /usr/bin/python /usr/bin/yas3fs --region $AWS_REGION --log /var/log/yas3fs.log --log-mb-size 256 --debug --s3-num 100 --download-num 100 --no-metadata --read-retries-num 5 --download-retries-num 10 --cache-mem-size 2048 --cache-disk-size 20480 --cache-check 10 --cache-path /mnt/cache/data --topic arn:aws:sns:us-west-2:$AWS_ACCOUNT_ID:$topicqueue --new-queue s3://$bucket /myapp/data

filesize=$(ls -l $input | awk '{print $5}')
maxstart=$((filesize/size-1))

dowork() {
    SECONDS=0
    i=0
    ptag=$1
    pinput=$2
    psize=$3
    pmaxstart=$4
    pchecksum=$5

    while [[ 1 ]]; do
        randint=$(od -vAn -N8 -tu8 < /dev/urandom | tr -d '[[:space:]]' | tail -c +2 | sed 's/^0*//')
        start=$((randint % pmaxstart))
        out=$(dd if=$pinput bs=$psize skip=$start count=1 2> /dev/null | $pchecksum | awk '{print $1}')
        i=$((i+1))
        printf "[Proc %-3s] [%3s] [%s] [%10s] [%s] %s\n" $1 $SECONDS $(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ") $i $out $start
    done
}

export -f dowork

seq 1 $workers | xargs -P $workers -n 1 -I {} bash -c "dowork {} $input $size $maxstart $checksum" &

sleep 5

echo 'Breaking...'

aws sns publish --region us-west-2 --topic-arn arn:aws:sns:$AWS_REGION:$AWS_ACCOUNT_ID:$topicqueue --message '{"default": "[ \"all-myapp-caches\", \"reset\" ]"}' --message-structure json

echo 'Waiting...'

sleep 2

pgrep -f dowork | xargs kill || true
pgrep -f yas3fs | xargs sudo kill -9 || true
sudo fusermount -uz /myapp/data
```

I attempted to rectify this with pull request #98 and this does ensure that on the initial 'rebalance' event the weights are exactly equal to target weights.

Ideally I wanted to ensure that at every 'rebalance' event, the realised weights are exactly equal to the target weights and the portfolio is fully invested.  The chart below shows the max and min weight across all assets and on inception the weights are equal:

![example](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/11697247/25886533/e5e4869a-3555-11e7-87d8-c2d7ad20f5ad.png)

After the initial 'rebalance' and the prices evolve independently, the commission will cause the individual asset weights to be slightly different and the weights will never be exactly equal.  I could add multiple bt.algos.Rebalance() events to algo stack and results will converge but this is very ugly, slow and there are not really real trades.

I could introduce a second algo which was called prior to 'rebalance' which would calculate the total comm prior to the rebalance and from the capital and then call 'rebalance' with no commission but this would be reliant on a linear commission function which was homogeneous across all the assets in  the portfolio so also perhaps not appropriate.

@pmorissette would be very interested in your opinion.

ok, then

```bash
git hub clone bob/awesome
git hub add upstream
git co github/that-branch
git co -b this-branch
# edits and commits
git lab create awesome
git push gitlab this-branch
```

All in all, what I really am saying is that you don't have the most common use case, and I don't believe this justifies changing *the default behaviour* which is aimed at 90% of the use cases:

* clone a repository, patch it, fork it, push it, make a PR

then maybe push to another git service transparently (between gitlab ↔ github …). That default behaviour being only how `git-repo` behaves when you don't give it arguments.

That being said, you can still do a lot with the `git add` command and its arguments to handle the way you want to use the tool. But you'll have to give it full arguments. For example, you got:

```
git hub clone hurrcorp/awesome
git hub fork # makes guyzmo/awesome on github
git lab create awesome # makes guyzmo/awesome on gitlab
# then you can add your team:
git lab add alice/awesome gitlab/alice --alone
git hub add bob/awesome github/bob --alone
git bb add charlie/awesome bitbucket/charlie --alone
git gt add donnie/awesome gitea/donnie --alone
git bb add eve/awesome bitbucket/eve --alone
…
```

of course, for each of the above, you'll need to have an account configured, or using git-repo won't help (because it's designed with having the possibility to interrogate the API of each service). Because, if you don't, well, you won't have the `git XX` alias configured (and for some of the commands the URL will be missing or with a non-sane default).

All in all, `git-repo` is a tool that's only a glue between what you can do on a git project management service and your local git repository. It's not meant to replace git. So you can do what you would like to do with git-repo, it's just not the default behaviour (so not automagical) and will require more typing than what you'd wish 😉

Phi/Gt is automatically generated in pydlm. In this special example, phi_1 and phi_2 will be multiplied to theta_1 and theta_2, so they are unidentifiable, i.e., (phi_1 = 1, theta_1 = 1) and (phi_1 = 0.5, theta_1 = 2) gives you the same result (similar for phi_2 and theta_2). Since theta_1 and theta_2 are the latent states and going to learnt by the DLM from the data, phi_1 and phi_2 will just be fixed to be 1 (because no matter what value you specified, it will be absorbed by theta_1 and theta_2 when fitting the model). 

For more sophisticated cases such as polynomial trend and seasonality, Phi_1 and Phi_2 will take more complicated forms rather than an identity matrix like in this case. In general, the transition matrix for each type of component is fixed a-priori in DLM. PyDLM will automatically generate these fixed transition matrix and combine them to construct the system transition matrix Phi. You don't need to worry about it or supply it yourself. For better understanding of the transition matrix (Phi/Gt) and the canonical form, you can read the chapter 7, 8 and 9 of the book Bayesian Forecasting and Dynamic Models (Harrison and West, 1999)

In most cases you don't even need to worry about phi_1 and phi_2. If you are interested in knowing the actual values, you can reference to the code under `pydlm/modeler/...` and look into each component, or you can query lively from the constructed model
```python
trend3 = trend(degree=3)
trend3.transition
```
If you do want to supply your own transition matrix (phi_1 and phi_2), you can directly modify when constructing the component.
```python
phi_1 = 1
phi_2 = 1
trend0 = trend(degree=0, discount=0.98)
trend0.transition = np.matrix(phi_1)
ar1 = autoReg(degree=1, data=y, discount=1.0)
ar1.transition = np.matrix(phi_2)
myDLM = dlm(y) + trend0 + ar1
myDLM.fit()
myDLM.plot()
```
But I highly recommend not doing this without a good understanding of the transition matrix in DLM. The transition matrix must satisfy some conditions (e.g., spectral_norm = 1) to be eligible. You can try some random transition matrix and see their weird behavior in the model fitting.

For the latent states (theta's) you can get them easily after fitting the model
```python
myDLM.getLatentState(filterType='forwardFilter')
myDLM.getLatentState(filterType='backwardSmoother')
```

Sorry, there is really no way to handle that error specifically. It's caught inside `telepot.loop.GetUpdatesLoop` (which is used by `MessageLoop`). But don't worry. The message loop will keep running despite the error.

Perhaps the webhook is receiving some [InlineQuery](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#inlinequery) or [ChosenInlineResult](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#choseninlineresult). [Update](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#update) may contain one of the three, with different keys. In other words, I think you should check whether the `message` field is present before passing it to the bot.

In case you haven't noticed, telepot has a **[webhook interface](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot#webhook-interface)**. Maybe you can consider that.

The way you call `bot.handle()` alarms me a little bit. I know little about CherryPy, but if it spawns a thread per request, that means your `bot.handle()` has to be thread-safe. You just have to be careful how CherryPy deals with many requests at the same time.

If you use telepot's **webhook interface**, then you don't have to worry about that, because it uses a synchronized queue to pass messages to the bot.

@badicsalex fixed. To reference the records:
```python
ipdb.routes[{'dst': 'default', 'family': AF_INET}]
ipdb.routes[{'dst': 'default', 'family': AF_INET6}]
```

Or, if the record is in some other table than the default:
```python
ipdb.routes.tables[tnum][{'dst': 'default', 'famliy': AF_INET}]
ipdb.routes.tables[tnum][{'dst': 'default', 'famliy': AF_INET6}]
```

Simple reference `ipdb.routes['default']` in such cases will return only one of the records, and it's impossible to predict, will it be IPv4 or IPv6.

The issue is that for IPv4, the kernel uses rtm_table=252 if RTA_TABLE>=256:
https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/net/ipv4/route.c#n2572
https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/include/uapi/linux/rtnetlink.h#n291

But for IPv6, the kernel uses rtm_table=(unsigned char)(RTA_TABLE) if RTA_TABLE>=256:
https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/net/ipv6/route.c#n3397

So this code doesn't work for IPv6:
https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/blob/master/pyroute2/ipdb/routes.py#L1064

@Gorialis I was having this same issue and your comment helped me fix it.
For anyone else having this problem, here's what I did:

```python
def start_loop(loop, gen):
    loop.run_until_complete(gen)


class DiscordAccessor:
    discord_loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
    client = discord.Client(loop=discord_loop)

    def __init__(self):
        self.channel_id = 'channelID'

        self.discord_coroutine = DiscordAccessor.client.start('token')

        self.discord_thread = Thread(target=start_loop, args=(self.discord_loop, self.discord_coroutine))
        self.discord_thread.start()
```

The event loop is set when initializing the client, and the separate thread blocks on the same loop. Otherwise, the client attempts to run on the event loop of the thread used to create it (client.py:142).

result = concurrent(...)

```python
result[key] = concurrent(...)
```

Here's what I've been doing lately. First make sure that your repo is up-to-date.

1. ``cd`` into the top directory (the one with ``setup.py`` and ``README.rst``)
2. type ``python -m rtv``

Explanation:
The ``-m rtv`` is shorthand for typing out ``python rtv/__main__.py``. When python sees the lines

```python
from rtv.main import main
main()
```

inside of ``__main__.py``, it will search the current directory for valid packages first, and then if it doesn't find anything it will start searching through site-packages and pip installs. Since the current directory has a folder called ``rtv/`` with an ``__init__.py`` inside of it, that is the one that python will import.

* with a `web-base-path` argument all routes of bottle (webui and API) will be prefixed

So we can have the following setup: 

* server 1:

* server 2: 
  * `web-base-path` on -> API available at `IP:61208/my-base-web-path/api`

Currently to connect to the server we execute : `glances -c <ip>`.

With the new `web-base-path` we need something like `glances -c <ip> --web-base-path <web_base_path>` or `glances -c <url_of_the_api>`

Today, iODBC supports most Unicode encodings -- UCS-2, UCS-4 (a/k/a UTF-32), UTF-8, and UTF-32 (a/k/a UCS-4).  UTF-16 support is expected in Q3 2018.

On macOS, native GUI ODBC interaction is only available with iODBC.  Native GUI apps and ODBC drivers that want that native Mac interface -- such as Microsoft Excel, and ODBC drivers from many vendors -- are therefore linked to iODBC (and typically, but not always, to the iODBC Frameworks rather than the dylibs).

It is somewhat odd that Microsoft chose to tightly couple their ODBC Driver for SQL Server for macOS to UnixODBC, given that Microsoft Excel and Microsoft Query are bound to iODBC, and their awareness that iODBC ships with macOS, but different teams there do not always communicate well with each other.  At the same time, it is worth noting that multiple ODBC driver vendors (including my own employer, [OpenLink Software](https://www.openlinksw.com/)) offer [ODBC Drivers for SQL Server](https://uda.openlinksw.com/odbc-sqlserver/) that are bound to iODBC.

For a more detailed Driver Manager comparison, you can check out [this Gsheet](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1jbcOfNgA5jXpYLo54kiIWRHcJp9kpH6j00pikpO93z0/edit#gid=0).

I think it ends up a little more and less complicated than that, because for a connection failure, the DB is immaterial.  pyodbc failed to connect (because of network problems or whatever) so the SQLSTATE can only come from the ODBC driver, if it is given any value upon connection failure not the DB.

MS SQL Native Client 11.0 and the MS SQL ODBC driver both set the SQLSTATE to 08001, when the connection fails.  Obviously, adding the detection of that SQLSTATE would require checking that SQLSTATE is the connection fail state for many, or all ODBC drivers.  It looks like the IBM DB2 uses this SQLSTATE as well, so maybe it is a standard feature of ODBC, given that connection failure is a relatively common occurrence.

However, there's still the matter of the unhandled exception.  For most database client applications, a connection fail causing the application and Interpreter to crash is a major bug, because it happens all the time in real production and development systems and the preferred action is to detect and mitigate rather than to explode and require user intervention.  I'm surprised that pyodbc is this old and has a bug in this basic of a functionality.  Surely no one failed to test it against the very likely case of being unable to connect to the database before me?  Perhaps there is another driver that would not have this issue and I'm happy to learn of such a driver if that is the case (for Windows, btw).

Thanks for looking into it.

I can't speak for FreeTDS, but with Microsoft ODBC Driver for SQL Server, queries with parameters will be executed using one of the system stored procedures, and since stored procedures are executed in a separate scope, temporary tables created there won't be accessible from outside that scope. Instead, you can try creating the table in the outer scope first:

```
 >>> cur.execute('SELECT * INTO #temptable FROM [Specimen] WHERE 0 = 1' ) # create empty table with appropriate schema
 >>> temptablequery = 'INSERT INTO #temptable SELECT * FROM [Specimen] WHERE [AccessionNumber] = ?' 
 >>> cur.execute(temptablequery, 'c1') 
 >>> con.commit() 
 >>> squery = ('SELECT * FROM #temptable') 
 >>> cur.execute(squery) 
```

I also added a bit of interesting code that rejects ambiguous strings (that match more than one pattern) but accepts those that end up being unambiguous (because the essential elements of the patterns are identical). One note, in  https://github.com/chadj/pyicloud/blob/ckdatabasews/pyicloud/base.py#L73, there are times when the server returns useful information in the response as json even though a 200 Response code was not returned.  So if the response was not OK but json was returned don't short circuit out of response handling.
See https://github.com/pokidovea/immobilus/pull/10

NeXpy uses `pcolormesh`, which expects the X and Y values to be pixel boundaries, not centers. In the previous function, `get_image` calls the function `boundaries`, which converts centers to boundaries - see  https://github.com/nexpy/nexpy/blob/master/src/nexpy/gui/plotview.py#L2593, which in turn calls  https://github.com/nexpy/nexpy/blob/master/src/nexpy/gui/plotview.py#L3823. If the grid is regular, I call `imshow`.

In [Slicer Package Manager PR](https://github.com/girder/slicer_package_manager/pull/55) we use [pytest-vcr](https://github.com/ktosiek/pytest-vcr) to intercepted and recorded the server responses.

It works well and is very easy to use. Just by adding the decorator `@pytest.mark.vcr()` on top of each one of your test you'd like to record the server responses, like for instance:
```
@pytest.mark.vcr()
def test_main():
    import github_release as ghr
    with pytest.raises(SystemExit) as exc_info:
        ghr.main()
assert exc_info.code == 0
```

Running the test for the first time will create a folder `cassettes` in `path/to/folder/github-release/tests` which will contain a file.yml for each tests decorate by `@pytest.mark.vcr()`.

For more information about this feature see [the pytest-vcr docs](http://pytest-vcr.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)

I think you could try to use that and see if it's compatible your testing. I could create a PR and try if you want!

Hi John,

You are right, this may be a very specific feature. 

We searched for a way to include ribbon definitions in the generated excel file which can trigger certain macro actions. In particular we generate excel files that are filled with a lot of values. The user can change these values and upload the file to a web application. Since there are a lot of values and many restrictions, validation on upload-time proved to be inefficient in terms of user experience. We wanted to improve UX by adding validation locally as a macro to the excel file. Executing the validation on every value change was not feasible because there are a lot of values and restrictions across them. Therefore we added an additional ribbon with a validation-button. 

However there are other different potential scenarios where such custom UI elements can be beneficial. As an example, a custom button in the ribbon could trigger some upload action that automatically sends information from the excel file to a webserver. 

What we did to support custom UI elements was to create such custom elements in excel and analyze the resulting xlsx file. It showed that these custom UI elements were added through separate xml files. Copying these xml files to another xmlx file replicated the UI. That's what we implemented in the library. A method to add xml definitions of custom UI elements. It may be too specific for your library. Furthermore it may be cleaner to generate such UI xml files by the library instead of copying them.

I'll ask a colleague to further describe on how one can obtain these UI xml files and what values they contain.

Regards,
Andreas

Great little utility, thanks. The watchmedo feature I was hoping for (and I think others too from rummaging around in feature requests), may be different than --drop, which is to say, if there are a batch of changes happening, I don't want the shell-command to be run until _after_ the changes have all happened, and only to happen once, as they are all the result of the same cascade of events. 

In other words I would like the system to wait for X milliseconds after each event to see if there's other events to be batched together with it, and then finally trigger the command after the first X milliseconds monitored without changes. You might call it --idletrigger

--drop seems to trigger the shell command immediately when the first change happens, and then ignore subsequent changes, which is quite a different feature set, highly likely to trigger race conditions, and a bit surprising that it's what people want, assuming they're using it for the kind of cascade commands I'm working with - recursively monitoring whole directories where a recompile has extensive knock-on changes which influence a lot of files, and where those knock-on effects may take quite a few milliseconds to fully propagate. It's not just the first change which should be noticed, or the ones which have raced to be completed in time, and the next pipeline should only be triggered _after_ all the changes are complete 

By all means tell me if my testing is wrong and I've misunderstood --drop. Also happy to refile a new issue if others subscribed to this this one really do want --drop and don't want --idletrigger Also I may have missed the full help or manual file as I can't seem to find a complete list of the commands available in my copy of watchmedo, so it may be hidden in another command line flag which I haven't examined, yet. 

    watchmedo --help

...doesn't list the --drop flag, for example, although it seems to be honored and...

    man watchmedo

...says the utility is undocumented in man.

Hi @bilalbayasut, my apologies for the late response. I appear to have missed your last comment.

You can create response pairs in ChatterBot using it's _training_ functionality. Here is a link to the documentation with more info and examples: http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/training.html

Essentially, training provides several different methods to add statements, as you described. For example, if I want to tech the chat bot that "Hi there!" is a response to "Hello", then I would do the following:

```python
from chatterbot.trainers import ListTrainer

chatterbot = ChatBot("Training Example")
chatterbot.set_trainer(ListTrainer)

chatterbot.train([
    "Hi there!",
    "Hello",
])
```

You can make that conversation of statements as long as you want, ChatterBot treats it as a single conversation. You can also create variations of the conversation by training on different data.

I hope this helps, please let me know if you have any further questions or if you think there is anything I could add to the documentation to help explain this better.

## Rant
Hello guys, personally would find it **very useful** to opt for a `ProcessPoolExecutor`. I have a project where I'm executing many requests that return very big JSON responses.

In my use case, the way memory allocation in python works makes those big JSON strings eat up a huge amount of system memory, which does not get released to OS even if I periodically `shutdown` the `executor` and force garbage collection. Using processes here would make the solution trivial since we would cycle processes instead of threads, releasing memory when each process finishes.

## Solution for pickling
It seems `session.request` "pickles" just fine today, and as for the `callback` function, it seems using `functools.partial` would do the trick. The only requirement for pickling the "partial" `callback`, is to make it an importable (i.e top level) function. Here is a working POC: https://gist.github.com/asfaltboy/ba33885f7fe08012d79b

P.s: will submit a PR, once I've added the tests.

If that's the case, maybe we should change the command to open the target file before asking for the wormhole code, to check for problems like this before consuming the code. On the other hand, we don't know what the target filename is until we've used the code and received the encrypted message from the sender (or even if we're receiving a file at all, since they might be using `wormhole send --text=MESSAGE`).

The problem is that setup.py requires setuptools. versioneer on the other hand doesn't require any package that are not included in python3.
This `version-script` executes in the context of the build, so by the time we call the version-script, setuptools is not installed.

We have discussed about a way to set the version at different times during the build cycle, not just at the start. But we don't want to add new features just because they sound cool, before they are really needed by our users.

@szhan if you still want to implement the `VonNeumann` topology: I have the feature for static and dynamic topologies (discussed above) almost ready for PR. If it is accepted you can implement the `VonNeumann` topology by inheriting from the Ring topology. In the constructor of the `VonNeumann` topology, you can use the `Ring` constructor to set it to "static". Then you can override the compute_gbest() method. Regarding the parameter `k` (used for the neighbour count in the Ring topology) you can use it as the range `r` (see link above). The number of neighbours is a Delannoy number as described [here](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Von_Neumann_neighborhood#von_Neumann_neighborhood_of_range_r) (it depends on the dimension and the range). I think it's the easiest solution if you just call `compute_gbest()` of the `Ring` topology with the corresponding new parameters in the `compute_gbest()` of the `VonNeumann` topology. I hope this description is understandable 😄.

> Is it necessary that I re-encode the audio to a specific bitrate? I'm using this on video files and it seemed to not mess with the bitrate when I left it alone.

It depends on your use case. Please note that the default output audio codec is PCM WAV, which is uncompressed, lossless audio. The default was chosen because it does not add any quality degradation compared to the original. It will result in large files (e.g., try normalizing an MP3 file and you will get a resulting file of ~10x the size).

If you want compressed audio output, you should choose a codec like AAC, by adding an extra option. For example:

    ffmpeg-normalize -vu -a aac -e "-b:a 192k" input.mkv

This will re-encode the file using the ffmpeg built-in AAC encoder.

It's quite possible that this is because `geoplot` is simply broken. It was never very well utilized, and hence, not terrifically well-maintained.

The timeline here is that after implementing this method experimentally in this package, I went and implemented a more general method, `aggplot`, in a package I published last January called [`geoplot`](https://github.com/ResidentMario/geoplot). That method is vastly better tested, and I believe also has significantly better performance characteristics. I also have not personally seen any uses of this geospatial plotting method "in the wild".

After thinking about this some, I decided to throw out the current `geoplot` method body and replace it with a thin interface to `geoplot.aggplot`. The existing methodology is a maintenance burden, `aggplot` provides a better implementation, and I doubt this will cause much user disruption. It's a backwards-incompatible change, but all things considered I'm comfortable making it.

The new method is now implemented. Let me know if you have any issues with it.

I've tried your tests again, there's still high max, even with the JIT disabled. I suspect these spikes in duration are caused by pypy's GC. On pypy `gc.disable()` doesn't really do anything, see http://pypy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/cpython_differences.html 

>    some functions and attributes of the gc module behave in a slightly different way: for example, gc.enable and gc.disable are supported, but instead of enabling and disabling the GC, they just enable and disable the execution of finalizers.

So I have a message object that is queued by my application server and once a client that is targeted to that object connects to the web server, that web server sends the message to the client.
I'm able to reproduce this issue even when having a small number of clients connected to my servers (at staging environment).

I use redis lock in my web-server just before sending a message to a client.
My usage with the redis-lock is pretty simple:
```
with Lock(redis_conn, str(msg.id)):

After implementing Flask-Security and getting it working, I merged with some other work and I suddenly got this error.
One difference was that the initial password for the admin user was changed from `password123` to `adminpass1234`

After a few hours of mocking around and the finding this issue, I came to the idea of switching back to the first password and voila, it worked!

Testing this with passlib directly gives the following results:
```
>>> from passlib.context import CryptContext
>>> myctx = CryptContext(schemes=["sha256_crypt", "md5_crypt", "des_crypt", "plaintext"])
>>> myctx.identify('testpassword1')
'des_crypt'
>>> myctx.identify('adminpass1234')
'des_crypt'
>>> myctx.identify('password123')
'plaintext'
```
So this clearly is an issue with passlib as @yueranyuan is onto.
Looking in the [passlib docs](http://pythonhosted.org/passlib/lib/passlib.hash.des_crypt.html) it says that a des_crypt hash is 13 characters long and it seems to identify any 13 character plaintext string as such.

I'm had play with your branch, looks great overall, I just have a few questions.

I like that you have preserved all the information about the log capture in a structured form. However this is going to massively bloat and effect the human readability of your produced reports. I've seem tests that can produce hundreds of logs in just their setup phase, this is going to dominate everything else. 

I think a hook where you can transform a LogRecord into a JSON object of your choice could be quite useful with your current implementation being the default.
Something like ```pytest_json_logtojson``` or ```pytest_json_logjsonserializer``` maybe.
I appreciate this would cause you loose the ability to reconstruct the LogRecord Object.

Actually it seems using a custom formatter to make the stdlib logging output JSON instead of opaque strings is not a new idea:
https://github.com/madzak/python-json-logger
However ```format``` is still expected to return string (even if that contains encoded JSON), so its probably not appropriate here.

You only capture stdout and logging in the case a failed test phase. I would like an option to capture everything regardless of outcome. There is still useful information in the output of a successful test, such as any form of subsystem profiling.

This is curious. 
I haven't checked with 1.3.7 but I am surprised it worked. In the whole text, it is very difficult to detect which labels are attached to equations (or align, or gather or eqnarrays, etc), and I only consider as equation labels the labels of the form `eq:something`. These labels are maintained in their respective environments while the others are replaced by an html anchor. The equation labels are then processed by mathjax. 
Since the label in your example equation doesn't begin by `eq:`, it might be replaced by an anchor and this could cause the mathjax processing to fail. Another issue is that `latex_envs` did not supported several `:` in labels (more precisely, the prefix in `eq:one:two` is detected as `eq:one` instead of `eq`; This small limitation is removed in this [commit](https://github.com/jfbercher/jupyter_latex_envs/commit/dca88fe1b2b1575d4e9f06bfd52a30b037999c5b).

Django registration never had migrations. In any case, if you want to do it, 2 migrations should be added: the initial one with the previous status before this code was merged and the one which applies the code changes in the model.

However, this case doesn't make any changes in the DB schema at all, because roughly a `OneToOneField` implements the same as a `ForeignKey(unique=True)`, with some extra [https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/ref/models/fields/#onetoonefield](utilities at Python level), not at DB level. When syncing a model with a `ForeignKey(unique=True)` or a model with a `OneToOneField()`, the resulting DB table rows will be exactly the same.

I'm actually not sure anyway why Django doesn't just pass this specific change when doing `makemigrations`, since the new migration doesn't add anything relevant. In fact, one can just fake it and the system won't notice at all, except for those parts in the code where Python is expecting a `OneToOneField`, of course.

In the first case you provide it could be like this

    print(droplet.get_events()) # use get_events everytime

There's nothing wrong with your second example. In fact this refactoring

    for droplet in manager.droplets:
        for event in droplet.events:
            print(event.id)

will work as expected and it is equivalent to

    for droplet in manager.get_all_droplets():
        for event in droplet.get_events():
            print event.id

The problem comes in when developers use droplet.events multiple times expecting the same result, expecting it to work like the other droplet attributes like `.name` `.size` etc. It's a matter of consistency.


This code is fine
    
    pairs = []
    for i in range(len(droplets.snapshot_ids) - 1):
        new_pair = (droplets.snapshot_ids[i], droplets.snapshot_ids[i + 1])
        pairs.append(new_pair)

where as this code

    pairs = []
    for i in range(len(droplets.snapshots) - 1):
        new_pair = (droplets.snapshots[i], droplets.snapshots[i + 1])
        pairs.append(new_pair)

will be slow (and incorrect if snapshots changes while this loop is running).

You're right it is a matter of preference and python is a very opinionated language (or maybe it's just me :P). To quote from the (Zen of Python)[https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/].

    Explicit is better than implicit.

    There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.

`get_events()` and `events` do the same thing, there should only be one and `get_events()` is more explicit and `events`.

@gmcquillan More specifically, the value being passed in by django-brake is not just an int, but it can be `0`. That's the root of the problem!

`brake/backends/cachebe.py:65`:

    cache.set(key, (count, expiration), timeout=int(expiration - time.time()))

Now when `expiration ~ time.time()`, then `timeout=0`. Django core (`BaseMemcachedCache.get_backend_timeout()`) turns this into `-1` (see my first post), which python-binary-memcached tries to pack into an 'unsigned long' to send it over the wire to memcached.

Unsurprisingly, packing `-1` into an unsigned long fails.

I think you're right that this isn't really django-brake's fault. After all, `timeout=0` is a documented case in Django's caching layer. However, it was the easiest to work around from django-brake, because there I could just prevent `timeout` from ever being `0`.

> Is this so that I can test against multiple python versions locally?

Yes, that's the *primary* purpose of using tox. The way it does this is by creating and managing virtual environments for *each* version of Python (or each environment, which may include a matrix of environments, much like Travis).

> How would I actually use this locally?

You'd run the `tox` command. I added some [documentation to the README](https://github.com/Shopify/shopify_python_api/pull/134/files#diff-04c6e90faac2675aa89e2176d2eec7d8) to show how to use this.

I just removed `pypy` and `pypy3` from the default environment set, since that could cause confusion. When you run the `tox` command it will test against each specified environment. In this case it will look for a Python 2.7 interpreter, a Python 3.4 interpreter, and a Python 3.5 interpreter, build an isolated virtualenv for each Python version, and run the tests in that isolated virtualenv.

> How does this specifically fix issues with people's local setup as in the issues you described?

I'm not 100% sure it will, but relatively confident. It's creating isolated [virtualenvs](http://docs.python-guide.org/en/latest/dev/virtualenvs/), separate from whatever other Python virtualenv they're working with. 

> Is tox a common utility in the python community?

Yes. The best part about adding tox support is that _no one is required to use it_. It makes it very easy to test against multiple versions of Python right away so you don't make incompatible changes, but it works by running whatever test script you feed it. Here it's running `python setup.py test`.

> I am a bit against adding more tooling here since it adds complexity and we're not python experts so simple is better!

This, then, is the type of tooling you _do_ want to add! No one wants to manually test against every supported Python version. It's simple to set up, there's no requirement to use it, and it lets you test against Python versions without having to think about it.

I'm not exactly sure what you're trying to do here... Assuming that you have three VMs on the same physical host set up as a Spark Standalone cluster, that cluster should be "shareable" as long as the various Spark configurations are set up. Ha that sounds like it is not needed (reverting - working together is fine :) ). Applying the previous state logic would just mean also evaluating the scheduled status and and re-scheduling those taskinstances everytime and let the taskinstance at run time figure out what to do. This is what the previous scheduler did with"none" states. That's kind of a one-liner. 

What I would suggest is to move the "set to scheduled state" to when it the taskinstance is sent to the executor. This resolves the orphaning when the scheduler is killed and is a small change. 

For the garbage collector we can add a time stamp last_updated to the task_instance which will get set everytime the record is updated (or a state change happens). The scheduler can then do a simple sweep by sql statement to set the state to "none" or to a new "reschedule" state by comparing it to an arbitrary timeout value. This would be orphaning down the line and would be future proof. Also a one liner. 

Or even better we could ask the executor which tasks it knows about and compare that to the list of scheduled tasks. If they are not there reschedule them. This is a little bit more work and might need to be combined with the above one as in the past the reporting by the executor was not really trustworthy. 

What do you think? Over the weekend I can have a patch for both I think.

Not only is there not a "most common" approach, there are conflicting approaches in "Python-land", specifically because Python-sqlite-land has one way to do this that is diametrically opposed to the Python-PostgreSQL-land way to do this and rather different from the Python-MySQL-land way to do this.

Often times, we get to _just_ let SQLAlchemy worry about it but, unfortunately, in this case SQLAlchemy doesn't have a standard way to do this. The best SQLAlchemy provides is using this problem as a [compiler example](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/compiler.html#utc-timestamp-function).

The discussion, essentially boils down to: 1) Should we calculate timestamps on the SQL server or should we calculate timestamps in Python on the individual workers, scheduler, and webservers; and 2) if we calculate timestamps on the SQL server, what should that code look like.

My assertion is that we should calculate the timestamps on the SQL server so that we can have a single, consistent clock for all timestamps.

This is def. a race condition in a short-lived process.  I think the send_multipart() zmq_callback gc is pending while the interpreter is shutting down.  We are canning the subsystem that is responsible for most of our context create/deletes, but there is def. a lingering issue there.

Yup, using blind pickle isn't a good idea if untrusted peers can connect to your sockets and send you messages. A note about it in the docs would be appropriate.

To be clear, `send_pyobj` is perfectly safe. Only `recv_pyobj` has the potential to be unsafe *if* untrusted messages can be delivered to your socket. This can be addressed with message signatures / etc. I feel like this is a pretty standard issue when dealing with messages on an untrusted network - make sure you validate messages before doing anything potentially unsafe with them.

Personally, I wouldn't recommend anyone use `send_pyobj` (or any `send_foo` functions) in anything other than toy examples. It's a convenience function for a trivial case, and any production deployment should be writing their own multi-frame message serialization.

Indeed, the cancel hookup is incorrect. The code assumes that a cancelled Future will have CancelledError as a regular exception, but unlike other failures, `.exception()` *itself* raises CancelledError, so cancel must be handled specially.

Fixed by #1115.

You may still want to have two sockets. Each socket can bind to one or more ports. Subsequent binds do not replace previous ones. The contexts aren't related to ports. With REQ/REP, you need to be careful that every send is followed by a recv and vice versa. That's the main source of hangs in such things.

I have a workaround for this which is OK but still requires a function call at the start of every run function. Would be better if included in luigi.

```
import logging
import luigi
from luigi.interface import build, setup_interface_logging
from multiprocessing import Queue
from logging.handlers import QueueHandler, QueueListener

q = Queue()

def run(tasks, *args, **kwargs):
    """ run tasks with queuelistener to handle logging """
    setup_interface_logging.has_run = True
    workers = kwargs.get("workers", 1) > 1
    if workers:
        log=logging.getLogger()
        listener = QueueListener(q, *log.handlers)
        listener.start()
    
    build(tasks, *args, **kwargs)
    
    if workers:
        listener.stop()

class Task(luigi.Task):
    """ redirect logging to queue """
    def __init__(self):
        """ add q to process """
        super().__init__()
        self.q = q

    def enable(self):
        """ call at start of each run process to initialise settings and redirect logging to queue """
        
        # for 1 worker leave settings alone
        log = logging.getLogger()
        if log.handlers:
            return

        # for multiple workers load settings but replace handlers with queue
        from logcon import log
        log.handlers = []
        log.addHandler(QueueHandler(self.q))

####### EXAMPLE USAGE #########################################################

class Test(Task):

    def complete(self):
        return False

    def run(self):
        self.enable()

        log = logging.getLogger()
        log.debug("running")
        log.info("running")
        log.warning("running")

        log = logging.getLogger("runlog")
        log.debug("running")
        log.info("running")
        log.warning("running")

        log = logging.getLogger("luigi-interface")
        log.debug("running")
        log.info("running")
        log.warning("running")

    def requires(self):
        return []
```

@ask, the change is that `download_file()` (which is a method to download an object from S3) is now multithreaded. The client creation/initialization that happens when creating a client via `boto3.client()` does not use threads.

More nuanced is that in previous versions of boto3, we had a conditional in boto3 that was roughly:

```
def download_file(self, ...): # downloads a file from S3
    file_size = get_file_size_from_s3()
    if file_size < 8MB:
        download_in_this_thread_in_one_api_call()
    else:
        use a concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor() and download the file chunks in parallel
```

This meant that in previous versions of boto3, if you stayed under 8MB, downloading a file would never spin up threads.  However, above 8MB and it seems like you'd still run into this problem in olde versions of boto3.

In the latest version of boto3, downloading a file from S3 via the `download_file()` method will always use threads.

We're still investigating if there's anything we can do on our end to improve this.

Hope that gives more context into what's going on.

import boto3

This is not the case: all that happens is your right to use, link to and distribute python-Levenshtein program is revoked until you come into compliance.

"To come into compliance" you must either cease to use, link to and or distribute the python-Levenshtein or provide your source code under the GPL license, with no extra restrictions. You as copyright holder can provide fuzzywuzzy source code under other compatible licenses if you wish. (eg you could make your part MIT, but you could not make it proprietary)

That should be sufficient for all but the most lawyery of lawyers.

Stallman would probably say you should:

1. Change LICENSE to COPYING.
2. Incorporate that text you've proposed for COPYING below into the top section of every file.

But the practical reality is that should anyone ever want to sue on this issue, it's your intent that matters more than anything else. You've established sufficiently to all but the most nitpicky that your intent was and is to release under GPL 2.0 by incorporating the LICENSE file in your code (as that's the established practice of probably at least half of Github users, and IIRC what their "use this license" feature adds to your repo for you. Anyone else should do their due diligence, see the GPL incorporated, and know that they're GPL-tainted (if that matters to them, it probably shouldn't) regardless of how strictly you follow the letter of the FSF recommendations.

When the [lockfile is read from the file](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/pipenv/project.py#L472), it just copies the structure over from the parsed TOML. I'm not sure why, but the TOML structure returns a specialized `ArrayElement` instead of a Python `list` (which is why `simplejson.dumps()` throws an error). Rather than iterate through the TOML coming out of the parser, it looked easier to have `simplejson.dumps()` convert the `ArrayElement`s to `list`s.

So pipenv never actually reached the installation step on `repoze.zcml`.  That's why it tells you it encountered an error while installing `repoze.workflow` -- it tried to lock and install that first, failed, and then told you about it. During the resolution step, it happened to resolve a different version of the second package which it hasn't even attempted to deal with yet.

By the way, you are using prerelease versions, which may depend on other prerelease versions, which means you probably want to pass `--pre` with your install.  Considering that package hasn't been maintained for 4 or 5 years, it's not that surprising that we can't properly resolve it in python3.7. I documented a lot of my difficulties because I'd love for it to be easier to contribute to this awesome project. I'll post a follow up PR mentioned below to fix some of the more procedural issues, as opposed to more structural decisions like pypa/pipenv#2557 (which you hint at with `pip install -e .` before testing) and the Makefile/Docker changes.

`make test` will run `run-tests.sh` (via docker-compose), but the linked image doesn't have python 3.7, so fails those tests. The Dockerfile is outside this repo, so can't be updated as readily. `docker-compose up` is usually used to start a service (that intends to keep running) as opposed to a command, which is usually run with `docker-compose run {service} {command}`, so the usage in the `Makefile` is a bit obscured.

I had tried to run `pytest`, but that brought in ~300 tests because the `build` directory after pip installing had them, which wasn't included in `pytest.ini` after `norecursedir` was rightly overridden to include the `vendor` and `patched` exclusions you listed. After updating `pytest.ini` to add back the defaults, an argument-less `pytest` had the same number of tests as your posted command above (185). The tests still take awhile to run, so running an individual test is nice, but see below on a caveat.

It's unclear how to add a test for a Pipfile with a new dependency. It's not particularly obvious that a local pypi clone is being used and new packages need to be added to `tests/pypi/`. Even after adding them, tests have to be run from the root repo directory for the local registry to find the packages (ex: can't run pytest from `tests/integration`) due to a bug in the pytest-pypi package or how it's passed `PYPI_VENDOR_DIR`. (Since tests take so long, I often try to run them from the folder containing the test I've just edited.)

I'll submit a follow up PR to fix the `pytest-pypi` package and `pytest.ini` and can add some testing documentation in `CONTRIBUTING.md` about how to add packages, etc. For the `Makefile`, it'd probably be nice to move the testing Dockerfile to this repo as `Dockerfile.test`, add python 3.7, and change the `docker-compose.yml` to build from that. Then maybe rename the `test` target to `test-all` and use `docker-compose run` instead of `up` and add a simpler `test` target that just runs `pytest`.

I want to point out how appreciative I am of this project and it's maintainers; it is now so much easier to manage dependencies, and I'd love to see this project continue to grow by lowering the barrier to contributing.

‎We have an additional use case in our project.  We have our own plugin system there.  Sometimes we want to rename a plugin but leave the old name as an alias for a few releases as a deprecation period.  To do that, we rename the file with the actual code and then we symlink to that file with the old filename.  Whenever our plugin system runs across a symlink, it knows that it's dealing with an alias and can do things like show the alias in the docs, display a deprecation warning when the plugin is used via the old name, etc.

If we had a way to mark these symlinks as not to be expanded or a way to toggle that behaviour for all files in the run we'd be able to use that to enable our use-case.

distutils currently hardcodes non-expansion of symlinks in sdists for tarfiles.  zip files expand the symlinks but it looks like a bug: https://bugs.python.org/issue12585  it looks like it's something people desire to make the same but currently there isn't an obvious fix for the bug so the behaviour is asymmetric between tars and zips.

It looks like even distutils expands the symlinks when installing the package.  So I'd probably still have to do more work to make it do what we need at install-time.

Our software requires a *nix like system so symlinks are supported all the places we care about.  This might not be the case for other projects.  being togglable in some manner would allow projects to choose but of course, then setuptools has to support both code paths.

@soupault no, our policy, which may or may not be enshrined in the docs [1], is not to mess with input data unless absolutely necessary. 

Secondly, we aim to preserve precision in our operations. If you want to preserve round-trip accuracy on `rgb2lab`, this means that Lab images have floating point values. If you want to have Lab values match the outside world, this means float values outside of the ~~allowed~~ recommended range:

```python
>>> color.rgb2lab([[[1., 0., 0.]]])
array([[[53.24058794, 80.09230823, 67.20275104]]])
```

So, we *must* be able to deal with floating point values outside [0, 1]. 

Finally, any function that works for float64 and throws an error for float32 is buggy. Support for one (at the API level) should imply support for the other.

You are potentially right that `preserve_range` should be unambiguous and ubiquitous wherever these issues are encountered. However `img_as_*` currently don't have this option.

.. [1] The closest I could find is this from our data types page: "we aim to preserve the data range and type of input images". It is not strong enough wording.

@jinty the bug you are seeing is described [here](http://bugs.python.org/issue15657).  The following patch fixes it:

```
--- a/src/zope/interface/_zope_interface_coptimizations.c
+++ b/src/zope/interface/_zope_interface_coptimizations.c
@@ -25,6 +25,15 @@
 #define Py_TYPE(o) ((o)->ob_type)
 #endif
 
+#if PY_MAJOR_VERSION >= 3
+#define PY3K
+#endif
+
+#ifdef PY3K
+/* See http://bugs.python.org/issue15657 */
+#define METH_KEYWORDS 0x0003
+#endif
+
 static PyObject *str__dict__, *str__implemented__, *strextends;
 static PyObject *BuiltinImplementationSpecifications, *str__provides__;
 static PyObject *str__class__, *str__providedBy__;
```

Some points worth noting:
- The reprs that are currently a bit unwieldy are things like `InterpolatedImage` that includes a full image as part of the repr, and objects with `rng` attributes, since the serialization of a `BaseDeviate` is kind of long.  So we might choose to add some ambiguity in those cases in return for brevity.
- I added a Transformation class as a type of GSObject.  This is now the return type from all transformations (shear, expand, withFlux, etc.)
- I haven't done the repr, str stuff for ChromaticObjects yet.  I think I'll want to add a ChromaticTransformation class there in parallel with the new structure for GSObject.  I think that will simplify a lot of the way transformations are currently handled there.  But I'll wait until #590 is merged, so I can incorporate the new Interpolation stuff properly.
- Other classes I haven't gotten to yet: FitsHeader, Catalog, Dict, PowerSpectrum, PowerSpectrumRealizer, PowerSpectrumEstimator, NFWHalo, Cosmology.  I think these are all easy.  I just haven't gotten to them yet.
- I completely rewrote the Shear class in the python layer.  We don't really ever need the Shear class in the C++ layer except to pass information back and forth, and there is nothing computationally intensive about it, so rather than do the boost.python pickling stuff, I just rewrote the class in python.  Also, I changed the fundamental data about the shear from (e1,e2) to a single complex (g1 + 1i g2), stored in an attribute called `_g`.  Most of the math about shears is a lot easier in terms of complex g than (e1,e2) (shear addition for instance!).  So no more `CppShear`.
- I got rid of the 2d interpolant stuff from the python layer.  We always just wrap 1d interpolants in an `InterpolantXY`.  We mostly insulated the user from this, but there were lots of places in the docs that referred to the 2d stuff that I think is just simpler to remove.  If we ever decide to have an interpolant that is not the same in x and y, we can bring it back, but until we have a use case for that, I think this is simpler.
- I got rid of the wrappings of the MultipleImageHandler stuff.  As Josh noted in #653, we don't ever use it, and there are no unit tests of it.  Again, I left it there in the C++ layer in case we decide we want it, but I don't think there is currently any need for it in the python layer.
- I added a zeropoint option to the Bandpass constructor.  Only a float value, so if you want to do something complicated, you still need to do `withZeropoint`.  But if you know the number, you can set it on construction.
- I added a redshift option to the SED constructor, so you can set that right away rather than doing `atRedshift`.

The EUPS magic wasn't too hard after all:
```
(galsim14) % source eups-setups.sh
(galsim14) % setup galsim
```
Note that the first line is finding `eups-setups.sh` in `~/anaconda/envs/galsim14/bin/` which is the first entry on `$PATH` in the `(galsim14)` environment.  The second line is finding `setup` from the same path, and creates the following `$PYTHONPATH`:
```
/Users/david/anaconda/envs/galsim14/opt/lsst/galsim/lib/python:
/Users/david/anaconda/envs/galsim14/opt/lsst/galsim:
/Users/david/anaconda/envs/galsim14/opt/lsst/stsci_distutils/lib/python:
/Users/david/anaconda/envs/galsim14/opt/lsst/python_d2to1/lib/python:
/Users/david/anaconda/envs/galsim14/opt/lsst/pyfits/lib/python:
/Users/david/anaconda/envs/galsim14/opt/eups/python
```
This prescription is specific to `bash`, but there are also `.csh` and `.zsh` scripts available to source.

To undo the steps above and get back to your original conda environment:
```

Ignore my last comment, I read the code too fast. All the session keyword arguments are passed on properly. When using SSL though you might want to make sure to include SSL specific settings. The following code works for me:
```python
from irods.session import iRODSSession
import ssl

context = ssl.create_default_context(purpose=ssl.Purpose.SERVER_AUTH, cafile=None, capath=None, cadata=None)

with iRODSSession(irods_client_server_negotiation = 'request_server_negotiation',
                  irods_client_server_policy = 'CS_NEG_REQUIRE',
                  irods_encryption_algorithm = 'AES-256-CBC',
                  irods_encryption_key_size = 32,
                  irods_encryption_num_hash_rounds = 16,
                  irods_encryption_salt_size = 8,
                  host = 'icat.example.org',
                  port = 1247,
                  user = 'rods',
                  password = '1234',
                  zone = 'tempZone',
                  ssl_context = context) as session:

    coll = session.collections.get('/tempZone/home/rods')
    print(coll)

sys.exit(0)
```
Which you can re-write as follows:
```python
from irods.session import iRODSSession
import ssl

context = ssl.create_default_context(purpose=ssl.Purpose.SERVER_AUTH, cafile=None, capath=None, cadata=None)

ssl_settings = {'irods_client_server_negotiation': 'request_server_negotiation',
                'irods_client_server_policy': 'CS_NEG_REQUIRE',
                'irods_encryption_algorithm': 'AES-256-CBC',
                'irods_encryption_key_size': 32,
                'irods_encryption_num_hash_rounds': 16,
                'irods_encryption_salt_size': 8,
                'ssl_context': context}


with iRODSSession(host = 'icat.example.org',
                  port = 1247,
                  user = 'rods',
                  password = '1234',
                  zone = 'tempZone',
                  **ssl_settings) as session:

    coll = session.collections.get('/tempZone/home/rods')
    print(coll)

sys.exit(0)
```

index 536daf9..e9b7a38 100644
--- a/manticore/core/smtlib/visitors.py
+++ b/manticore/core/smtlib/visitors.py
@@ -354,7 +354,7 @@ def constant_folder(expression):
     global constant_folder_simplifier_cache
     simp = ConstantFolderSimplifier(cache=constant_folder_simplifier_cache)
     simp.visit(expression, use_fixed_point=True)
-    clean_cache(constant_folder_simplifier_cache)
+    #clean_cache(constant_folder_simplifier_cache)
     return simp.result
 
 
@@ -519,8 +519,12 @@ class ArithmeticSimplifier(Visitor):
         if isinstance(arr, ArrayVariable):
             return
 
-        while isinstance(arr, ArrayStore) and isinstance(index, BitVecConstant) and isinstance(arr.index, BitVecConstant) and arr.index.value != index.value:
-            arr = arr.array
+        if isinstance(index, BitVecConstant):
+            ival = index.value
+            # props are slow and using them tight loops should be avoided, esp when they offer no additional validation
+            # arr._operands[1] = arr.index, arr._operands[0] = arr.array
+            while isinstance(arr, ArrayStore) and isinstance(arr._operands[1], BitVecConstant) and arr._operands[1]._value != ival:
+                arr = arr._operands[0]  # arr.array
 
         if isinstance(index, BitVecConstant) and isinstance(arr, ArrayStore) and isinstance(arr.index, BitVecConstant) and arr.index.value == index.value:
             return arr.value
@@ -543,7 +547,7 @@ def arithmetic_simplify(expression):
     global arithmetic_simplifier_cache
     simp = ArithmeticSimplifier(cache=arithmetic_simplifier_cache)
     simp.visit(expression, use_fixed_point=True)
-    clean_cache(arithmetic_simplifier_cache)
+    #clean_cache(arithmetic_simplifier_cache)
     return simp.result

The `DTEND` defined in RFC 2445 is used only to define the end time of a single event... not the end date/time of an rrule. `Recurrence.dtend` is a completely different concept that happens to share the same name. In the context of django-recurrence, it exists to have parity with django-recurrence's handling of `dtstart`, meaning that specifying `dtend` is just supposed to allow a user to explicitly specify the last occurrence. To be clear, it is completely made up for django-recurrence. It is not part of any spec.

*Note: When reading this is would probably be helpful to have `template_generator.py#_convert_defnition` open next to you, I refer to variable names and specific code branches*

I am going to try and add a `cls` argument to the `TemplateGenerator._convert_definition` method. 

This `cls` argument will represent the troposphere class that the `definition` argument represents. 

If it is `None` it means either:

1. `definition['Type']` does not exist
  1.1. Not an AWS resource (Probably a Parameter, Function, ect)
2. `definition['Type']` is not a type represented by any troposphere classes
  2.1 Processing a resource with a property named `Type` (For example a CodePipeline `Source` resource)
  2.2. Custom resource type in form `Custom::`

If `cls` is not `None` and the provided `definition` is an AWS resource (aka the `Type` field is present in the `definition` argument) then the `expected_type` variable will be set to `cls`. This value of `expected_type` will then be passed to `_create_instance` method. 

Then by passing a `cls` argument when calling `_convert_definition` in these places:

- `TemplateGenerator.__init__` when processing `cf_template` `Resources`
- Recursively in `TemplateGenerator._convert_definition`
- `TemplateGenerator._create_instance` & `_normalize_properties` `None` for all usages

This should prevent the Template Generator from seeing any field named `Type` and going off and trying to create an instance, as the decision to do so is now up to the caller of `_convert_definition`.

Ha! @fdr , you're a genius. It shouldn't be a surprise but it turns out the issue had nothing to do with wal-e after all. My full restore_command was as follows:

```bash
restore_command =  '. /etc/bashrc ; AWS_REGION=ap-southeast-2 wal-e --terse --s3-prefix=s3://mybucket --aws-instance-profile wal-fetch "%f" "%p" 2>&1 | egrep -v "not a lzop file"'
```

What I hadn't realised was that egrep returns a non-zero status if wal-e doesn't produce the string "not a lzop file" ... which it won't do when --terse is turned on, obviously.

The solution was a slightly less brain-dead restore_command:
```bash
  restore_command = '. /etc/bashrc ; o="`AWS_REGION=ap-southeast-2 wal-e --terse --s3-prefix=s3://mybucket --aws-instance-profile wal-fetch "%f" "%p" 2>&1`" ; r=$? ; echo "$o" | egrep -v "not a lzop file" ; exit $r'
```

Thanks for pointing me in the right direction, much appreciated.

Ok, I think I'm starting to see what you're doing now.

So big thing would be to make them step by step, one commit per *atomic* piece of functionality. That would include a new unit test (*very* occasionally two are needed) and the code that makes the test pass. Generally these are in the context of a series of commits of this sort getting an acceptance test to pass. This might be different because it sounds like it's purely oxml infrastructure you're adding. I can take a closer look at that a bit later.

This atomic step commit process makes the commits easy to review, validate, and ultimately merge. If you review the commit history a bit I think you'll readily recognize the pattern I'm describing. Look for a commit that begins with `acpt:` and the several that follow it. You'll find that over and over.

Can you mention what you wanted w:shd for? The bigger picture view? There may be some work that was done in python-pptx that shares this part of the XML Schema (it may be part of the DrawingML (DML) schema shared by Word, PowerPoint, and Excel).

Generally we haven't added oxml infrastructure by itself, only in support of API calls. This is generally good agile practice, but it also allows us to skip testing the oxml infrastructure separately since it can easily be combined with the API call tests and actually produces more robust tests that way.

So it's probably worth sketching up an analysis document to describe the API calls you're looking to support and what the Word behavior is, how it maps to the XML vocabulary, etc. Those are here: https://github.com/python-openxml/python-docx/tree/master/docs/dev/analysis/features
and look like this for example: http://python-docx.readthedocs.org/en/latest/dev/analysis/features/styles/index.html

It's worth reviewing a few of them, they're not very standardized yet, but if you browse through you'll start to get the gist. The main purpose is just to work out what the intended functionality should be, especially the API calls (since those are forever once committed). When collaborating on functionality like this they bear a greater burden, needing to bridge two minds rather than just the one.

In general you want to avoid formatting changes to existing code, unless there are bits that clearly depart from PEP-8 or current codebase conventions. It's noisy in commits and everybody has different preferences, so when there's not a clear right answer it's generally easier to go with the local convention. And it's less likely to raise an unnecessary dispute when getting your code merged. Better to raise it as a question first if you think a wholesale improvement is called for and then concentrate it all in a single commit.

Hi David -- these are unit tests, rather than acceptance tests. python-docx has a two-level test strategy, basically unit and integration levels. The acceptance tests are an integration test, exercising the capability from the API all the way down and back and using actual Word files instead of the stubs and so forth that make sense at the unit (isolated) level.

In general, the acceptance tests *drive* the unit tests; in the sense that the unit test to be developed is determined by where the acceptance test currently errors out. So the acceptance tests come very early, right after analysis and before implementation.

That said, these look good. I can take a closer look when we get to that stage. In the final commit sequence these would show up in separate commits, each with the code that makes that test pass.

Let me know if you need more :)

In #3594 we decided to use the command 

```bash
kolibri manage content movedirectory
```

At the risk of bike-shedding that decision, I wanted to propose one convention update before committing to it:

> Multi-word commands should be `snake_case`.

In this case, the command would become `kolibri manage content move_directory`

Mashing multiple words together without any clear separator makes them hard to read, which is especially problematic because we won't be internationalizing the commands any time soon.

(In this case, the command scans to me like "moved irectory")

The caveat is that Marathon actually returns all tasks from all apps bundled into the event data so in my case where I got 200 tasks you can imagine the size of the buffer.

> If we count 1 byte per character, 500k characters (or iterations of 1 char) would be...500kb of data ! OMFG !

In marathon's sse case, the last character will always be the EOF so the code from sseclient would have to iterate over the whole buffer trying to find it until it finally encounters it. 

It would be even easier to read the whole buffer and use a simple (yet powerfull) string search with ".find()" or "in".

Ill try to post some benchmarks here, but I think it will be magnitudes faster than 1/2 million searches using regexp.
