The Ruby binding returns an object which is immediately usable.  Furthermore, compare the error handling of the two libraries:

docker-py:
```
In [25]: cli.pull('someimage/that:doesntexist')
Out[25]: u'{"status":"Pulling repository docker.io/someimage/that"}\r\n{"errorDetail":{"message":"Error: image someimage/that not found"},"error":"Error: image someimage/that not found"}\r\n'
```

docker-api gem:
```
[7] pry(main)> Docker::Image.create('fromImage' => 'someimage/that:doesntexist')
Docker::Error::NotFoundError: No such image: someimage/that:doesntexist
from /var/lib/gems/2.1.0/gems/docker-api-1.26.0/lib/docker/connection.rb:46:in `rescue in request'
```

Docker-py returns a string of newline-delimited JSON strings that contain values intended for human consumption.  To use this I'll have to parse these strings searching for keys which I don't know.  The docker-api gem throws an exception which is immediately and easily handleable as an error.

As Docker-py is an API binding I expect the output of it's methods to be easily machine parsable, in my opinion that's the whole point of using the binding: to make coding against the API easy.  Returning data representations which must be massaged into a form which is machine parsable simply isn't good design for a binding.  Please use this issue to improve your return formats.  Thank you.

Thanks! This works great. I did it like this:

```python
import six, uuid
import versions.models

class MyVersionable(Versionable):
    def new_identity(self):
        self.id = self.identity = six.u(str(uuid.uuid4()))
        self.version_start_date = self.version_birth_date = versions.models.get_utc_now()
        self.version_end_date = None
        return self

    class Meta:
        abstract = True
```

I am not sure what to call this method: it doesn't make a duplicate; instead, it changes the current object into its own duplicate. And I didn't want to call `save()` because I've ended up in tough situations in the past trying to customize the `save()` method. So I called it `new_identity` because it gives the current object a new identity.

Happy to do a PR if you are OK with this method name, or suggest a different one.

I think this needs more discussion.

We currently consider all remote resources to be safe. The issue with this is when uploading a datapackage to, for example, a CKAN instance using `ckanext-datapackager`. The CKAN instance will load the datapackage and its resources if it's safe. We only consider local resources to be safe it they're relative to the `datapackage.json` path, so a user couldn't send a datapackage with a resource as `/etc/shadow`, for example. However, as all remote resources are considered safe, they could still send a datapackage pointing to `http://10.0.0.1/secret-file.txt` in the server's local network and have its contents published on CKAN.

This is kind of difficult to exploit, as it requires knowledge of the server's intranet and the ability to upload a datapackage to CKAN. This is only considering CKAN, though. There might be uses of `datapackage-py` that makes this hole easier to exploit.

We could limit remote resources to the same base path of the `datapackage.json`, the same way we do with local resources, but that means that it won't be possible for a datapackage to point to resources outside of it domain (e.g. the World Bank data portal).

I think it's OK to consider all remote resources as safe, as we do now, maybe just documenting this possible attack vector so clients can handle it. What do you think, @pwalsh @roll @rgrp?

Hi there @Spotlight0xff 

Thanks for sending a PR to fix this. It's interesting to learn that QNX exhibits this behaviour. I'm wondering, however, if there's a better way to fix this. Prior to your change (on all systems that return a non-`None` value) the value of `name` would always be text (not bytes). Now there are edge-cases that consumers must handle where `name` is almost always text but on a very small portion of systems (probably less than 1%) it will unexpectedly be bytes. Do you have thoughts on how to handle this inconsistency for users? `chardet` should always provide the same consistent interface to its users. In some cases, with this change, users will see the filename in other cases they'll see the filename preceded by `b'` and followed by `'`. That's far from ideal.

Thanks for working with us on this.

Cheers,

Now answering some specifics:

> A single class for each of light, fast and regular which know how to perform the syncing logic for each of those operating modes. LightSync, FastSync, FullSync ?

There's actually only two modes -- light and full. When doing a full sync we might [start with a fast-sync if we are too far behind](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/654) but once we've caught up we switch to regular-sync. That is handled by `FullNodeSyncer`

> A single class for both light and full which exposes the full networking API (currently handled by ChainSyncer and LightChain). 
>  - Does not implement syncing logic.
>  - Exposes APIs for sending and receiving data over the network
>  - LightClient, FullClient
>  - LightSync gets access to LightClient to perform networking tasks.
>  - Both FastSync and FullSync get access to FullClient for network interactions.

I think this is well encapsulated in peer (`ETHPeer` and `LESPeer`) and protocol classes. The sync services already delegate to those for network requests

@jannikluhn FYI

This is a common pytest pitfall.  `xfail` is intended for tests that are unreliable, rather than tests that are *required* to fail.  A test marked with `xfail` that passes doesn't cause the test suite to fail.

In cases where we have an explicit failure, I'd rather stay away from `xfail` and instead write the test with a `with pytest.raises(TheExpectedError)` in the place that it's expected to fail.  Then at least when we fix the error, we'll have a clearly failing test so that we know that section needs to be updated as well.

In general, I think we should stay way from `xfail` entirely because it's not that useful.

Simply removing it changes nothing at all. All it is, really, is an empty package, but with a list of dependencies. Those dependencies cause specific versions of Python, NumPy, etc. to be installed.

The problem is that those dependencies restrict the upgrades you can do. You cannot just upgrade Python without also upgrading all of the packages Anaconda points to.

So removing anaconda will give you more flexibility. And if you ever want to put it back, you can, but it will "snap" all of the packages to a particular anaconda release.

Ping! Just wanted to +1 this again as I've come across another situation where I need this functionality.

In this instance I *don't* want to install Python from `conda-forge` so would like to be able to exclude it from my `update --all` whilst still pulling in the latest packages from any of my configured channels.

This could also be resolved by allowing packages to be pinned to a specific channel(s). I didn't see an issue for this so I opened #2978. I think the functionality requested there is complementary to this and that both would be very useful to have.

You are also running a very old version of conda.  So start with `conda update conda`.  If you keep having issues, file a new issue with the new stack trace.  Also, please always include the output of `conda info`.

You didn't really hear it from me, you heard it from [here](https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-recipes/pull/129#issuecomment-337147043), we just follow expert advice in these regards (I know very little about MPI).

When I say we should deprecate it I do not mean that we should do *anything* regarding changing legacy packages that identify it as a dependency.

What I mean is that future packages and future versions of existing packages should replace any declared `mpich2` dependency with `mpich` when they are rebuilt.

So this is not related to any conda concept of `conflict` or this `@` stuff.

I SSH'ed into the test machine. One obvious issue I found is that `/opt/wakari/anaconda/pkgs` had none of the packages required to reproduce the environment. Conda will never be able to clone without either the tarballs or the extracted packages residing in that directory. We do not just copy the package files from the original environment---they are re-linked _from the package cache_.

However, @dorzel is basically correct. `/opt/wakari/anaconda` cannot be cloned offline with 4.1.11, because it was constructed with an older version of `conda`, and that version of conda did not embed the necessary metadata in the environment to complete the cloning. I _am_, however, able to do an offline clone of a _clone_ of that environment. That is, I can do this:

1. Update conda to 4.1.11
2. `conda create -c wakari -p ~/new_env --clone /opt/wakari/anaconda`
3. `conda create -p ~/new_env2 --clone ~/new_env`

Note that the second, offline clone does not require `-c wakari`.

My recommendation is that `/opt/wakari/anaconda` be recreated using the latest 4.1.x version of conda, so that the necessary package metadata is in place to do the cloning. 

I did come up with a couple of fixes that would make it possible to clone the existing `/opt/wakari/anaconda` environment offline. It basically involves allowing offline mode to use the _cached_ metadata for the channels. Whether we want to do this now for 4.1.x, include those changes in 4.2.x or later, I leave up to @kalefranz.

However, in either case, the package cache _must_ be filled. There is absolutely no way to accomplish an offline clone with `/opt/wakari/anaconda`  in the state that I found it in.

Yeah, but the ls command doesn't imply it is specific to files--it "lists directory contents".  Where as a command called "list" under a group called "files" by definition implies it will list files.  Similarly, commands like ls make it easy to get just directories via -d or grepping the output.

I think there is a larger consistency issue:
- All other list commands in the CLI enumerate all resources of the parent command group type.  For example, vm list only enumerates vms; it doesn't enumerate scale sets.  
- A common pattern in the CLI is to create an item, then subsequently retrieve it via the show/list command.  It's weird to use a command from one part of the CLI to create something then grovel through the commands elsewhere in the CLI to retrieve it.

If nothing else, I'd consider adding a parameter to files list that only returns directories and adding help to the directory commands to tell users to use the file commands to get directory info.

Here  is a small python script that does this: https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/examples/pybullet/examples/inverse_kinematics_husky_kuka.py#L71

`def accurateCalculateInverseKinematics( kukaId, endEffectorId, targetPos, threshold, maxIter):
	closeEnough = False
	iter = 0
	dist2 = 1e30
	while (not closeEnough and iter<maxIter):
		jointPoses = p.calculateInverseKinematics(kukaId,kukaEndEffectorIndex,targetPos)
		for i in range (numJoints):
			p.resetJointState(kukaId,i,jointPoses[i])
		ls = p.getLinkState(kukaId,kukaEndEffectorIndex)	
		newPos = ls[4]
		diff = [targetPos[0]-newPos[0],targetPos[1]-newPos[1],targetPos[2]-newPos[2]]
		dist2 = (diff[0]*diff[0] + diff[1]*diff[1] + diff[2]*diff[2])
		closeEnough = (dist2 < threshold)
		iter=iter+1
	#print ("Num iter: "+str(iter) + "threshold: "+str(dist2))
	return jointPoses`

Now Content-Range is also provided when HTTP 416 is returned.
`file.size() == 200`
client request:`headers['Range'] = 'bytes=500-'`
server response:`headers['Content-Range'] == 'bytes */200'` <- tell client the actual size/allowed range

I guess you refer to using a Notebook server on Windows.
You can try to execute the Notebook server with a VSB script:

```vbs
Dim oShell
Set oShell = WScript.CreateObject ("WScript.Shell")
oShell.run "C:\Anaconda\python.exe C:\Anaconda\Scripts\ipython-script.py notebook", 0
Set oShell = Nothing
```

Setting `intWindowStyle` parameter of the `object.Run(strCommand, [intWindowStyle], [bWaitOnReturn]) ` to 0 results in hiding the window. [Details for Windows Script Host](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/d5fk67ky(v=vs.84).aspx)

The only drawback is that you will need to kill the notebook server using a Task Manager (loop for the Python executable) or using pskill or similar.

Great, glad to see conversation about this topic! I've been tracking it for [some time now](https://mail.scipy.org/pipermail/ipython-dev/2012-December/010839.html), and am really interested in how this round turns out! I was indeed convinced then that make a cell type for everything is not the answer... but perhaps it is time to start thinking about some of the UI pieces around rich output that haven't fundamentally changed since then. 

Background
For a while now, I've been making [magics](https://github.com/bollwyvl/jademagic)/[extensions](https://github.com/bollwyvl/nb-wysiwyg)/[widgets](https://github.com/bollwyvl/ipytangle)/[tricks](https://github.com/bollwyvl/nb-mermaid)/[fancies](http://bollwyvl.github.io/quarto/) to get closer to authoring particular kinds of wysiyMean, ala lyx/prosemirror. We've come a long way, if you are willing to jump through many hoops!

Front End Stuff
+1 common mark as a container for robot-authored Stuff (JS, HTML, CSS, SVG), even if we have to push forward the maths. If you read/write markdown and all those things, you're kind of a robot, so you should be fine.

Even if adopting commonmark for all that Stuff, and getting our sanitization house in order, the authoring of said Stuff in the browser (in the notebook) probably does need some love. 
- CodeMirror has our back, and has [completion](https://codemirror.net/demo/complete.html) and [linting](https://codemirror.net/demo/lint.html) for all of those, and many of the transpiled languages which we'd want to see with full support of npm.
- This would be backed by the nascent "front end ʞernel", and is what we'd need anyway to make JupyterLab into the best place to build Lab/Notebook extensions.
- Just ditching out to the text editor would lose the immediacy of being in-browser...   
  - and heck, maybe there's something to this stuff-embedded-in-json idea: there is something kind of beauitful in the [vulcanized](https://github.com/Polymer/vulcanize) polymer components: here's your nbextension... as a notebook!

User-authored text
The exception to this is, I think, user-authored complex prose documents.

If we were to embark on this path, I think it would end up having:
- typography-focused approach to the UI of rich text authoring
  - much like @rgbkrk's demo (:heart: it!) fade out all the UI, embrace the keyboard shortcuts, and start writing your journal paper. or letter. or contract. or whatever.
- a document-oriented data structure for the content, likely via prosemirror...
 - footnotes!
 - sidebars!
 - comments!
 - images with captions!
 - numbered equations!
- a no-fooling, extensible layout engine ([nbpresent](https://github.com/Anaconda-Server/nbpresent) just hacks the css)
 - parpers, posters, banners
- robust themes that look publication grade out of the box

Then the question is... are these then even cells? or is this another, prose-native view of your notebook you have chosen, which can include cells embedded in _it_. Do you show them next to each other? Does this even go in an ipynb, or is this a separate file type altogether, or a wrapper around both kinds of file, or a PDF with a local file store?

Very exciting stuff, and hopefully a topic for the dev meeting!

@pytest.fixture(scope='module')

    def delete_conf():
        mgmt_root.tm.util.bash.exec_cmd(
            'run', utilCmdArgs='-c "tmsh delete sys config file pre_run"')
        mgmt_root.tm.util.bash.exec_cmd(
            'run', utilCmdArgs='-c "tmsh delete sys config file post_run"')

    def teardown():
        mgmt_root.tm.util.bash.exec_cmd(
            'run', utilCmdArgs='-c "tmsh save sys config file post_run"')
        check = mgmt_root.tm.util.bash.exec_cmd(
            'run', utilCmdArgs='-c "diff /var/local/scf/pre_run '
                               '/var/local/scf/post_run"')

            delete_conf()
        else:
            delete_conf()

    request.addfinalizer(teardown)
`````

1. Drop foreign key:
```SQL
ALTER TABLE catalogue_productimage DROP FOREIGN KEY catalogue_productima_product_id_49474fe8_fk_catalogue_product_id;
```
2. Run migrations
3. Restore the key:
```SQL
ALTER TABLE catalogue_productimage ADD CONSTRAINT `catalogue_productima_product_id_49474fe8_fk_catalogue_product_id` FOREIGN KEY (`product_id`) REFERENCES `catalogue_product` (`id`);
```

an alternative to doing this, each time you update your library project:
```
rm -rf ~/.nuget/packages/MyLibrary
dotnet pack ../src/MyLibrary
dotnet restore -f ../src/MyLibrary/bin/Debug
```

is to version your library higher with each change, by putting this in your library project.json : `"version": "1.0.0-*"`

Then using `dotnet pack --version-suffix 000X ../src/MyLibrary` where X increases with each change. 

For the cli we use the commit count of the repo to keep versions increasing.

which describes what should be returned in response to each kind of challenge. There's also the list of alternative implementations

This particular issue tracker is meant for tracking issues with our Python client implementation, so I think a community forum thread would be a more appropriate place to ask about this kind of issue (if you encounter a bug where the CA side appears not to be following the spec, you can file it at https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder/issues). @jbrockmendel The problem with this and many of your other attempts at structural changes to the parser is that they are piecemeal and tweak around the edges, which is exactly what I *do not want*. I will need some time to learn more about the general theory of parsing before I'm ready to make any decisions about how the parser will change, unfortunately.

Unless you want to present a fully-featured parser as a *fait accompli* with an interface I would be happy with, I imagine most of these suggestions and PRs will languish for a while. I would probably not recommend this (unless you prefer to just write a separate parser for reasons other than getting it merged into `dateutil`), as we seem to have markedly different sensibilities about what makes for a good API.

I am, generally speaking, wary of doing parsing in the lexer. I think part of the problem is that `timelex` seems to actually just be a tokenizer, not a proper lexer (e.g. a tokenizer that attaches some additional context to the tokens). I don't understand the various techniques of lexing and parsing and when to apply them well enough to know what the right solution is here.

Also, what exactly is the point of pre-processing these like this? Both the tokens and the separators are available to the parser, and it frequently re-combines them as necessary. Why is this case special such that we should violate this separation of concerns?

OK, so this change to cloudpickle tries to detect whether a function (being pickled) might access (via attribute) a module contained within a package. If so it instructs for the module to be re-imported on unpickle. (Cloudpickle already instructs to re-import the top-level package, but this is not always sufficient to re-import required child modules.)

An alternative to doing this would simply be to document, that all functions to be pickled must import dependent modules as top-level objects in the namespace (i.e. ask the user never to `import foo.bar` except `as foobar`). However, it is in the spirit of cloudpickle to try to be more magic than ordinary pickle (e.g. not asking the user to refactor-out lambdas etc).

There is a test that the function can be unpickled and executed in a fresh new process. (Thanks @ogrisel for suggesting the encoding.) There is also a pair of tests that use the original process (which is trickier to test because must undo imports). It is a pair because cloudpickle handles globals and closures slightly differently.

I think the best route is to treat `XXX with ABC` as an "atomic" symbol when parsing an expression and evolve the code such that both the license and the exception symbol can be obtained from this. By atomic I mean that this symbol could not be further simplified in a boolean simplification. This would also mean that the exception could only be applied to a single license and not a whole expression. The reason to make this is simplicity and correctness. The implication is that:
- `(gpl and lgpl) with gcc-exception` would never be a valid expression: an exception can only apply to a single license and not a whole expression
- `gpl with gcc-exception and lgpl with gcc-exception` could be a valid expression and would not be simplified further as it is equivalent to `XXXX and YYYY` as `lgpl with gcc-exception` would be treated as a whole symbol.

Really appreciate the feedback @wholmgren and @cwhanse, I wasn't trying to bring this up as a 'please do this' but more of a 'what do you think' of either of the two options I presented. 

1. External libs just try to copy `ForecastModel` function signatures for non Thredds/NetCDF servers; or
2. pvlib provides a base class decoupled from Thredds/NetCDF to allow for different data sources and formats allowing external libraries to create implementations compatible with pvlib users.

I think 2. in the long run would allow pvlib maintainers then make choice on a case by case which implementations are worth pulling into pvlib.

@wholmgren I agree with what you are saying, but I think the simplicity of the current design to be able to just create a `GFS` model and class `get_processed_data` is a powerful one, plumbing of data sources and data structures is time consuming, tedious and error prone. It would be nice to continue that simplicity by enable other developers to create additional data sources for something like `ForecastModel`. As maintainers, you can then pick and choose which ones should be baked into pvlib guided by whatever values/rules you've decided.

In anticipation of the upcoming Santa Clara workshop, I'm consolidating the lists in the comments above into a new list. I also removed some of the items that don't apply to python. It's probably best to start making new issues for specific features.

- [ ] pvl_louche - Estimate Direct Normal Irradiance from Global Horizontal Irradiance using the LOUCHE model
- [ ] pvl_orgill_hollands - Estimate Direct Normal Irradiance from Global Horizontal Irradiance using the Orgill and Hollands model
- [ ] pvl_erbs - Estimate Direct Normal Irradiance from Global Horizontal Irradiance using the Erbs model. And from v1.3: Corrected pvl_erbs; the coefficient of K_t^2 changed from 4.3888 to 4.388
- [ ] pvl_reindl_1 - Estimate Direct Normal Irradiance from Global Horizontal Irradiance using the Reindl 1 model
- [ ] pvl_reindl_2 - Estimate Direct Normal Irradiance from Global Horizontal Irradiance using the Reindl 2 model
- [ ] pvl_martinruiziam - Determine the incidence angle modifier using the Martin and Ruiz incident angle model
- [ ] pvl_adrinverter - Anton Driesse's Grid-Connected PV Inverter
- [ ] pvl_PVsyst_parameter_estimation - Estimates parameters for the PVsyst
  version 6 module performance model.
- [ ] pvl_desoto_parameter_estimation - Estimates parameters for the De Soto
  single diode module performance model.
- [ ] pvl_rectify_IV_curve - Ensures that Isc and Voc are included
  in a IV curve and removes duplicate voltage and current points.
- [ ] pvl_calcparams_CEC - Create module performance model coefficients
  for the single diode model used by the CEC.
- [ ] pvl_calcparams_PVsyst - Create module performance model coefficient
  for the single diode model in PVsyst version 6.
- [ ] pvl_huld - Calculates DC power using the Huld PV module model.
- [ ] pvl_calcPwat - Calculate precipitable water (cm) from ambient
  air temperature (C) and relatively humidity (%). see #115.
- [ ] pvl_FSspeccorr - Calculate spectral mismatch modifier based on
  precipitable water and absolute airmass. see #115.
- [ ] pvl_getISDdata - Fetch data from NOAA ISD at ftp.ncdc.noaa.gov.
- [ ] pvl_readISH - Read data fetched from ftp.ncdc.noaa.gov into a table.
- [ ] Changed pvl_calcparams_desoto to require effective irradiance as its
input, rather than absorbed irradiance (i.e., broadband irradiance
reaching a module's cells). pvl_calcparams_desoto no longer computes and
applies a spectral mismatch modifier.
- [ ] Changed pvl_calcparams_desoto to remove the requirement that all inputs
be vectors of the same length.  Inputs S and Tcell can be vectors of the
same length; all other inputs should be scalars.
- [ ] Changed pvl_erbs, pvl_louche, pvl_orgill_hollands, pvl_reindl_1, and pvl_reindl_2 to output DNI, DHI and Kt, and to clarify each model’s description.
- [ ] Corrected description of pvl_kingdiffuse to clarify that the King diffuse model estimates total plane-of-array diffuse (sky diffuse and ground reflected irradiance).
- [ ] Added CEC module library and inverter library from SAM2015.6.30 in .csv format and in .mat format, along with scripts to read the .csv files and write the .mat files.

From the standard:

> remainder: x - y * n, where n in Z is nearest to x/y 

The integer nearest 7/4 = 1.75 is 2, so rem(7,4) = 7 - 4 * 2 = -1.

Table IX in the paper translates `fp.rem` to *remrne*, which is similarly defined on page 10.

This has come up during differential testing.  Consider the following input:

```
(set-logic QF_FP)
(declare-fun x0 () Float32)
(assert (= (fp.abs x0) (fp.min (_ +zero 8 24) (_ -zero 8 24))))
(check-sat)
```

Z3 reports `unsat` here, whereas MathSAT5 reports `sat`, with a model that corresponds to +0.0.  Z3 returns -0.0 for the `fp.min` operation, and since the absolute value of a value cannot be negative, Z3 ultimately reports `unsat`.

Based on the [comment from @LeventErkok above with the snippet from the standard](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/68#issuecomment-98777443), I'm leaning towards Z3 being incorrect here.  From the standard, it's clear that -0.0 and +0.0 are possible results for the `fp.min` use above.  However, my interpretation is that the correct behavior is to nondeterministically pick one of the inputs, as opposed to deterministically picking a particular input, which would lead to implementation-defined behavior in the solver.  The standard mentions different hardware platforms that have chosen different strategies, and so the lowest common denominator to me seems to be nondeterministic choice.

Some of these problems look legit at first glance, others may be because of unclear semantics, e.g., `sat::clause::operator` may be reported because of the zero-size array sat::clause::m_lits. (See also the two warnings that are disabled in the Microsoft compiler ealier in that file.)

`m_signal_sent` is needed because we need a way of determining if a signal was **actually sent**. This is because according to `pthread_cond_timedwait()` it may spuriously be woken up.

From `man pthread_cond_timedwait`
>  When using condition variables there is always a Boolean predicate involving
shared variables associated with each condition wait that is true if the thread
should proceed. Spurious wakeups  from  the pthread_cond_timedwait()  or
pthread_cond_wait()  functions may occur. Since the return from
pthread_cond_timedwait() or pthread_cond_wait() does not imply anything about
the value of this predicate, the predicate should be re-evaluated upon such
return.

Here the "Boolean predicate" is the `m_signal_sent` variable that I have added.

The `records_pkey` error that we can see in the mentioned Sentry error is indeed easy to catch, since it's a conflict on `(id, parent_id, collection_id)`, and not on timestamp unicity (`idx_records_parent_id_collection_id_last_modified`).

However, how should we react to it? If it is the same user on both ends, then do nothing or overwrite is fine. But if it's two different users, then the second one should probably receive a unicity error.

I feel like there's another larger issue here which is that we should only support storing true JSON records -- meaning, we should have barfed when the hashed password (as `bytes`) tried to get stored to Postgres. Is there an option for ujson to let us *not* serialize bytes, but only the usual booleans, integers, floats, strings, lists, dicts, and None?

The update addresses the "two copies" scenario I was concerned with, so now I'm descending to the next level of detail at this point (i.e. naming)...

I can't help but think it'd be a lot more straightforward to just have:

```python
def version():
    return '0.9.0'
```

* TL;DR: It's simpler :)
* As a blanket generalization, I'd argue making it a simple method translates to a more uniform interface across languages (e.g. I'm thinking of C++ and Go)
* In reality, I'd argue fairly strongly it is not a burden to implementors to have to implement this method! :) Worrying about an abstract base class just to avoid implementors having to implement a method returning a fixed string, IMHO, adds abstraction complexity and no actual, real-world value.  Also, I'd argue, OpenTracing API versions shouldn't be changing *that* often that keeping this value up to date should be any maintenance burden (note: this claim does *not* apply to the *implementation version* which in theory changes with every push by the implementator)
* `OPENTRACING_PYTHON_API_SEMVER` seems awkwardly verbose: the OPENTRACING and PYTHON part should be self-evident. I think that it's a *semantic* version is a documentation point (and I tend thinking using straightforward, full words is better than abbreviations when it's possible - i.e. `version`). 
* I also think including "API" in the name is unnecessary given the presence of something called `implementation_id` (this is harder to objectively argue, but I prefer the short name and would be surprised if consumers of OpenTracing found `version` confusing). That said, I believe I would still find a way to sleep soundly at night if it was called `api_version` :)

All and all, I think `Tracer.version()` would be simpler but I realize it's a large part just opinion and am not overly concerned about this.

Yeah, I agree with @nfelt's listing of design decisions to be made. To elaborate a bit on the first point (the behaviors of chart sliders with regards to the global one), tags within a run (such as train or eval) often share the same steps because we often log, say, eval or train data at the same frequencies. However, steps may differ across runs. One might write train summaries every 5 steps, but eval summaries every 100 steps. Perhaps when the global slider changes, chart sliders could round down to the nearest step to the global one (and the UI could make that clear).

I unfortunately also can't find a precedent PR for introducing a global slider. Indeed, #471 (introducing one slider per run for PR curves) is probably most relevant.

Yeah, that's true - that is the other option, is to explicitly run the summary ops in one `sess.run` call and then the update ops in a separate `sess.run` call.  In a way, that's the most obvious solution, and gives you some extra flexibility (e.g. you can record summaries at a step 0 before any updates, and then record them after the update for steps 1...N).  But I didn't want to recommend that in general because separate `sess.run()` calls might mean re-executed shared nodes unintentionally, which could be an extra performance cost or in the worst cases lead to multiple updates (if any of your summaries ops depend on stateful updates that are also depended on by the update op).

From a practical standpoint; this allows OT to manage multiple tx buffers in the future and submit the specific buffer that it wants to TX. For example a router could allocate a buffer for its beacon, populate it one time and then reuse it with a couple mods to the temporal fields.  Meanwhile a separate buffer would be used for the less consistent frames.  Secondly, this allows for the possibility of a future transmit queue and backoff logic to exist in the platform layer.

From a stylistic reasonl; it was agreed that it is more intuitive for the API to have passed to it, the buffer to be transmitted, rather than to infer that it is the same buffer as the one that was provided in the call to otPlatRadioGetTransmitBuffer.  The reason we have this API is that some hardware have limitations on which buffers may be used for transfer and this allows the driver to avoid having to copy the buffer into another buffer in order to transmit it.  It was not obvious that calling this API multiple times would yield the same buffer object.  In fact that may not be true in the future.

@jskeet I think the configuration should be OK. I see that this method is marked `request_object_method: true`. This is supposed to mean that you can pass optional fields like [`service_filter`](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/master/google/devtools/clouderrorreporting/v1beta1/error_stats_service.proto#L62) by constructing `ListGroupStatsRequest` explicitly and passing that to the method. The general strategy we've been observing, AFAIK, is to make constructing the request message the _only_ way to pass optional fields, although technically we could add another flatting config to generate a method overload that takes `service_filter` as a parameter.

Hi, 

It is because you can't re-run a code that is building a graph. In TensorFlow, you only need to build your graph once, if you re-run the cell, it will append again the operations to the default graph (so you graph will have 2 inputs, and then 3 and then 4 etc...)

Ideally, you only need to run a cell once, otherwise you have specify a new graph every time you call that function:
```
def _deep_classifier(features=200):
    with tf.Graph().as_default():
        net = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, features])
        net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 64)
        net = tflearn.dropout(net, 0.5)
        net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 10, activation='softmax')
        net = tflearn.regression(net, optimizer='adam', loss='categorical_crossentropy')

def deep_sentiment():
    global data
    model = tflearn.DNN(_deep_classifier())
    X, Y, _ = data
    model.fit(X, Y, show_metric=True, run_id="dense_model")
    return model

```

Note: Every DNN model has its own tf.Session, so to share weights variables along multiple models, you need to pass that session as argument of DNN class:
```
model1 = DNN(_deep_classifier())
model1.fit(X, Y)

# Initialize a new DNN model with same session as model1
model2 = DNN(_deep_classifier(), session=model1.session)
model2.predict(...) 
```

The way I first implemented it was something like this: (I changed it around so that I removed company private code and this was way before I started contributing to dask so my knowledge was far less than it is today)

```python
import numpy as np
import dask.bag as db

from toolz.curried import frequencies
from toolz.curried import valmap
from toolz.curried import merge_with
from toolz.curried import unique
from toolz import merge_with as _merge_with


def normalize(_dict):
    return {k:v/sum(_dict.values()) for k,v in _dict.items()}

corpus = 2*[
    "a a b b b",
    "a a a a c",
    "c b a d e f g",
    "a b b b c c c d d d a b",
    "c b a d e f g",
    "a b b b c c c d d d a b"
]


base = (
    db
    .from_sequence(corpus, npartitions=4)
    .map(str.split)
)

tf = (
    base
    .map(frequencies)
    .map(normalize)
)

idf = (
    base
    .map(unique)
    .map(frequencies)
    .reduction(merge_with(sum), merge_with(sum), split_every=2)
    .apply(normalize)
    .apply(valmap(np.log10))
    .apply(valmap(np.negative))
)
```

This has a draw-back, since it must pass over the entire dataset once in order to calculate the `idf` step.

To me a better solution (which is what I want to implement here) would be first to implement the [hashing trick](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.feature_extraction.text.HashingVectorizer.html#sklearn.feature_extraction.text.HashingVectorizer) and then use the [tfidf](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.feature_extraction.text.TfidfVectorizer.html#sklearn.feature_extraction.text.TfidfVectorizer).

You should get the same speedup from avoiding redundant computation either way. And they should work on any scikit-learn estimator so your custom transformers should be fine.

Feel free to continue asking questions in this issue if you run into anything. Ok, I think this is a fairly minimal example:

```python
def f(*args):
    pass


dsk1 = {
    'X0': 0,
    'y0': 0,
    'b0': 0,
    'b1': (f, 'b0', 'X0', 'y0'),
    'X1': 1,
    'y1': 1,
    'b2': (f, 'b1', 'X1', 'y1')
}

dsk2 = {
    'Xy': 0,
    'X0': (f, 'Xy', 0, 0),
    'y0': (f, 'Xy', 0, 1),
    'b0': 0,
    'b1': (f, 'b0', 'X0', 'y0'),
    'X1': (f, 'Xy', 1, 0),
    'y1': (f, 'Xy', 1, 1),
    'b2': (f, 'b1', 'X1', 'y1')
}
```

**dsk1** This is handled well. The blocks of `X` don't have a common ancestor. This is the "shallow" case, and we make the right choice.


![mydask](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1312546/41206572-9ac9c662-6ccb-11e8-919f-7f244e3cff24.png)


**dsk2** is not handled well. The blocks of `X` (and `y`) share a common ancestor. We choose to go down the outer edge first 

![mydask](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1312546/41206573-a1f4e836-6ccb-11e8-9eb3-e4c2a008e4d0.png)

Next, add a constant to the template parameters of the tuple caster: ``int ImplicitConversions = PYBIND11_IMPLICIT_CONVERSIONS``. This ensures that the types are different (i.e. no ABI problem).

Finally, a higher level remark: the terminology of just calling this an implicit conversion in the code is a bit confusing, as there is already a kind of implicit conversion (and e.g. all the ``type_caster::load`` functions have a template parameter ``convert`` which enables/disables just that.)

> B isn't identified as garbage in the garbage collection pass, because its reference is held by pybind11.

`B` won't be as long as `A` is referenced elsewhere, but once `A` is identified for gc, the weakref is cleared which admits both the weakref itself and `A`, so the list, `A`, `B`, and the weakref all get deallocated in the same gc pass.

```Python
from keepalive import A, B
import gc

gc.collect()
gc.set_debug(gc.DEBUG_STATS | gc.DEBUG_COLLECTABLE)

lst = [A(B())]
lst.append(lst)
del lst

gc.collect()
gc.set_debug(0)
```

gc: collectable <list 0x7f6a4099abc8>
In B::~B()
In A::~A()
gc: done, 3 unreachable, 0 uncollectable, 0.0006s elapsed

> Is there a way to determine whether a given py::handle is pybind-managed?

`pybind11::detail::get_type_info(Py_TYPE(h.ptr()))` returns a `detail::type_info *` if `h` is an instance of a pybind-registered type, `nullptr` if it isn't.

Thanks @slavonnet. This is a bug that has been reported by others and will be fixed: https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/chinese-idn-issurance-requests-malformed/21528/5.

What's happening here is that [Nginx uses PCRE as its regex engine](http://nginx.org/en/docs/http/server_names.html#regex_names), while Certbot uses Python `re` to check if something is a regex and match against it.

PCRE supports named capture using both Python and Perl 5.10 syntax. This example uses Perl syntax.

To solve this problem in the short term, you can switch to using Python named capture syntax, which both Nginx and Certbot support (this means adding the single character 'P'):

```server_name ~^(?P<sname>.+?).ace.bigroominternet.co.uk$;```

In the longer term, we should probably automatically support this use case by switching to a more modern regex engine (which it seems might eventually be solved for us [when Python changes its built-in engine](https://stackoverflow.com/a/7066413)). What we should definitely 100% ****not**** do is attempt to check when this one special case is happening, and stick in a hacky solution that might fix it sometimes but will probably break in other ways.

For now, Certbot only supports Python-style named capture syntax.

It is not possible natively.

You could write a custom ``ComplexParameter`` for this:

```python

from factory import declarations

class RecursiveStopper(declarations.ComplexParameter):
    def __init__(self, default_depth, recursive_field, stopper_value=None):
        self.default_depth = default_depth
        self.recursive_field = recursive_field
        self.stopper_value = stopper_value

    def compute(self, field_name, declarations):
        depth = declarations.get(field_name) or self.default_depth
        # use depth + 1: if depth is 0, we must stop now => set FIELD_NAME=None
        override = '__'.join([self.recursive_field] * (depth + 1))
        return {override: self.stopper_value}
```

With this, your factory would look like this:

```
class MyFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory):
    class Params:
        next_depth = RecursiveStopper(10, 'next')
        previous_depth = RecursiveStopper(5, 'previous')
```

Fairly new to coding/deep learning/pytorch/vision, so hope I am doing this right. Here is what I found:

1. 650eb322c91ee65a8f487d12beb3e5fc6a41223f
ToPILImage was originally added for testing purposes. Only requirement at the time was ToTensor(ToPILImage(tensor)) returned the identity, because it was used in testing other transformations. Originally took a FloatTensor, converted to byte array, and passed into fromArray() without "mode" being a part of the API
2. https://github.com/bodokaiser/vision/commit/683852d2737dd61fdeac2d069b3ac11f46caaa1d
First commit to add the concept of mode to the code, but doesn't change anything related to this bug. 
3. https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/122/commits/b8e69d8379e73b3bf6a16254c353aa193c5c4f8a (Mar 23, 2017)
This commit introduced the concept of passing in a mode, in order specify what logic to apply. 
4. https://github.com/pytorch/vision/commit/901c1ad2645e6994a33f41ee11828cc613de72cc (Oct 17, 2017)
This commit added checking to verify that the passed in mode matched the final output mode. This was not previously a requirement, and is why we are having issues here. 

From 1-4 the API has been kept so that passing in a float from 0-1 with mode=None was a requirement. Since #4 (Oct.17,2017) passing in a FloatTensor with mode='F' has been broken. 

My thought on options
1. Since mode='F' has been broken for awhile now, I think we can possibly have different behavior here. When mode='F' is specified we allow the range to be 0-255 or whatever is appropriate for Image.fromArray(). Maybe give off a DeprecationWarning for mode=None and revisit it at a latter date/version?
2. Break API and switch both mode='F' and mode=None. This is mainly due to the fact that the behavior of mode=None and mode='F' are inconsistent with the rest of the API currently. Also, mode=None currently assumes mode='F' behavior, which is a bit strange according to how the API evolved over time. 
3. Turn off the check added in #4 for mode='F' only. This would be most consistent with the API from the time between #3 and #4

What is the preference on the fix? Either way would be interested in trying to code this one if it isn't too much of a problem.

All, we have had some internal discussions at Berkeley of whether the batch API should be asynchronous or synchronous:

In the async case
```with ray.batch() as mybatch:
  a = f1.remote()
  b = f2.remote()
  c = f3.remote(a, b)

fun4.remote()```
from the user's viewpoint the batch "finishes" as soon as `f3.remote(a, b')` is submitted, and hence `fun4.remote()` can finish before `f3.remote(a, b')`. (From the system perspective however the batch always completes only after the last function in the batch finishes, i.e., after `f3.remote(a, b')` finishes.)

In the sync case, `fun4.remote()` is submitted only after `f3.remote(a, b')` finishes.

Also note that in the asynchronous case, one can still force synchronous execution by using `ray.wait()`, e.g.,

```with ray.batch() as mybatch:
  a = f1.remote()
  b = f2.remote()
  c = ray.wait(f3.remote(a, b))

fun4.remote()```

Any comments give your use cases?

> Is it possible to force mpegdash/hls 720p 50fps stream with streamlink

I'm not sure whether streamlink currently supports MPEG-DASH streams; youtube-dl does, but only for VOD, not live streams (dynamic manifests). 
If you are willing to do some manual labour, then you can grab yourself the AdobeHDS manifests and/or AppleHLS playlists and feed those to streamlink directly...
 
 E.g. for BBC One (London), one can sniff the mediaselector URL to the streams: 
`http://open.live.bbc.co.uk/mediaselector/5/select/version/2.0/mediaset/pc/vpid/bbc_one_london/atk/78ca5063d64bb5b308d1d87f737b610aa2db0f3f/asn/1/` 
and the hds manifest for the Akamai CDN is 
`http://a.files.bbci.co.uk/media/live/manifesto/audio_video/simulcast/hds/uk/pc/ak/bbc_one_london.f4m`

If you substitute `bbc_one_london.f4m` with `bbc_one_hd.f4m`, then 
`streamlink -o BBCOneLive.flv "hds://a.files.bbci.co.uk/media/live/manifesto/audio_video/simulcast/hds/uk/pc/ak/bbc_one_hd.f4m" best` =>

```

st)
[cli][info] Opening stream: 5166k (hds)
[download][BBCOneLive.flv] Written 312.3 KB (1s @ 213.9 KB/s)

If you prefer AppleHLS streams, then for

one gets (Akamai CDN):

Hi there,

Thanks for the interest and the feature request!

This is something I have been considering adding for the ssh2 client so good to see there are real world use cases for it.

It will only be for the ssh2 client, not paramiko, as paramiko currently [does not support SCP](https://github.com/paramiko/paramiko/issues/150). 

The scp.py module above is a wrapper over a remote `scp` command on the target host and therefore requires the `scp` binary to work. That is opposed to [this project's goals](http://parallel-ssh.readthedocs.io/en/latest/introduction.html#design-and-goals) of providing a library with no external utilities/binaries requirements.

The ssh2 library does have SCP support so this feature is feasible for that client only.

As for monkey patching, can already run `scp` remotely via the existing `run_command` functionality, to do what [scp.py](https://github.com/jbardin/scp.py/blob/master/scp.py#L143-L145) does if that is what you want. There are lots of edge cases that come up with just a cursory glance of that module (non utf-8 filepaths? Windows directory paths?) - in addition to it relying on a binary - that make it unusable for general use cases, though you may decide you are ok with that for your needs.

Sub-classing single or parallel clients to add functionality not already there is another option.

There is no way to allow pluggable operations without them either being specific to the client library or relying on remotely executing a binary, both of which can already be done via the two above methods.

Some documentation on how to extend functionality would be useful though so I think that is worth doing. Please feel free to contribute with your use case if you can.

@lababidi you are correct, with the unique folder, we would not need to keep track of individual files. The down side is that separate instances of Goose would not be able to leverage already downloaded images. I actually started implementing option 2 this morning after thinking about it a bit more. 

What I have so far allows for the following syntax:

#### Standard format
``` python
from goose3 import Goose

sites = [site1, site2, site3,...]
goose = Goose()
for site in sites:
    goose.extract(url=site)
goose.close()  # it will attempt to close out when garbage collected also
```

#### Context manager
``` python
from goose3 import Goose

sites = [site1, site2, site3,...]
with Goose() as goose:
    for site in sites:
       article = goose.extract(url=site)
```

#### Multithreaded
Also, one could do the following to parallelize the extraction while still leveraging the images downloaded by other threads - this is rough pseudo code and I have not tested any race conditions:
``` python
import threading
from goose import Goose

def worker(sites):
    goose = Goose()  # Could also use a context manager
    for site in sites:
        article.extract(url=site)
    goose.close()

threads = []
all_sites = [site1, site2, site3, ....]
for i in range(5):
    t = threading.Thread(target=worker, args=([all_sites[x:x+seg_length] for x in range(0,len(all_sites), seg_length)]))
    threads.append(t)
    t.start()
```

Switching back to option 1 will not allow different threads to use the same files. It is kinda a cart before the horse issue, do we care about that use case?

There are two issues with this approach:

1. Our setup.py is doing incremental build, but we don't take environment variable as dependencies, so changing environment variable doesn't trigger a rebuild of the proto files. (This is the reason causing the CI failures right now).
2. We check in the generated proto files into the repo, if we build with a namespace value other than the default one "onnx" it will makes the proto files shown as modified in the working copy. (This one should be abled to solved by changing the gen_proto.py to genereate the proto files outside of the source directory.

ok that might be doable, though I confess I haven't tried doing anything like this (i.e. training with purely live data) before, so definitely listen to others if they chime in here. I think you'd just need to use an off-policy algorithm, like DDPG or whatever as you may not be easily be able to break this into "episodes." The trick would be in getting your reward function defined correctly and maybe also set this up in a way that each snapshot can see a window of historic sequential data leading to the present since there may be a pretty big time delay between action and reward here.  The simple way is to put some time offset columns in your state (CO2 level now, one minute ago, two minutes ago, etc), or you can do something with recurrent neural nets / LSTM where you provide those past observations in a 3d array. I don't know the best way to set up your reward function off hand. You can go a couple different ways. Either everything's a penalty (i.e using electricity, exceeding CO2 threshold), or you can do something more complex. Either way, how you weight the cost of electricity vs CO2 levels will definitely play a role in the training speed, but the feedback loop with tweaking those might be too slow to be practical, so you'll probably just want to pick something reasonable and let it run.

I think one thing that could help with all this would be if you were able to pilot this idea in several buildings at once and do an asycn off policy method. Like A3C or D3PG/D4PG (which hopefully will be added to this repo soon). Then have each building be controlled by a different actor with some additional state variables involved about the overall system specs (system capacity, size of building, and maybe a one-hot vector to identify each building, etc.). That way you can have a critic that's learning a more general value function that informs the policy and hopefully it all could learn faster. I just don't know how ok it would be if this thing isn't working correctly in several buildings for a couple months while it learns.  You might need some backup system here, or have your environment force a fan on at some really poor CO2 threshold to ensure there's a backstop at least.
